sources.title: The Shapeshifting Luthy Block on Route 66
sources.content:
You know, there's a specific kind of hunger you get when you're driving through the Arizona desert. It's not just uh needing lunch. It's this primal need for something substantial. So, imagine you're cruising down Route 66. You pull into Kingman, Arizona, and you stop at 401 East Andy Divine Avenue.

You walk into a place called El Palasio. It's vibrant. There are murals everywhere. It smells incredible, like roasted childs.

And then you see it on the menu, the El Bandido.

The Bandit, a name that implies eyes. It might be getting away with something illegal.

Honestly, it should be. We're talking about a 28 in burrito. It's a cylinder of food the size of a toddler.

I mean, it's stuffed with sirloin, chicken tenders, rice, beans,

and just when you think they're done, they shove a whole tamalei and a chili rieno inside of it.

It's a culinary nesting doll,

but it's also a structural marvel.

It feeds four people, or as our notes say, two firefighters. But here's why we're talking about a burrito on a show about deep history.

While you're trying to navigate this absolute unit of a meal. You're sitting inside what might be the most deceptive building in the American West.

H this isn't just a Mexican restaurant.

No, it's a 140year-old time capsule that has been disguised, burned down, rebuilt, and masked over so many times that it tells the entire story of the West was one, lost, and you know, rebranded.

That is the perfect way to frame it. We're doing a deep dive today into the Luthy Block. And the mission here is a bit different. We're not looking at a battle or a famous treaty. We're looking at one specific corner of one specific railroad town

and we've combed through the National Register of Historic Places nomination forms, architectural field guides, and local histories from folks like Jim Hingley.

And the argument we're making today is that buildings aren't static. They're shape shifters.

Exactly. We tend to think of architecture as stone and brick permanent.

But the story of the lu block proves that a building is actually a living organism. It changes its skin to survive. It went from a pioneer pharmacy to a prohibition social hub to a Chinese cafe to a Route 66 roadside attraction.

And if you know how to read the walls, you can see the ghosts of all those eras sitting right there next to you while you eat that burrito.

So, let's peel back the paint. We need to go back to the very beginning. And I mean the Wild West beginning, 1880s Arizona territory.

It's a harsh environment. No air conditioning, scarce water, and a lot of dust.

And into this dust walks a man named HH Watkins. He's our first protagonist. He's a businessman looking to set up a pharmacy.

But when I was reading his background, I had to stop and reread it three times because his father BF Watkins wasn't just any pioneer. He was a survivor of the Donner party.

That is a detail that carries a massive amount of psychological weight.

For those who might have slept through history class, remind us the Donald party is the pioneer tragedy, right?

Oh, it's the defining nightmare of the westward expansion. Winter of 1846. A wagon train gets trapped by snow in the Sierra Neadas. Starv sets in. And as most people know, they resorted to cannibalism to survive.

And HH Watkins is the son of a man who lived through that. See, this is what I want to unpack. We usually treat that as a piece of trivia. Oh, fun fact. His dad was in the Doner party,

right? But it's not a fun fact.

Think about the mindset of a guy raised by a survivor of that event.

It reframes everything about his character.

It does. If your family history involves surviving the absolute worst case scenario of the American frontier, moving to the Arizona desert, to sell medicine doesn't seem that scary.

It seems like an opportunity. It suggests a level of I don't know genetic grit, a survival at all costs mentality.

And we see that play out in how Watkins handles his business,

right? Because he doesn't start in Kingman. He starts in a mining town, Mineral Park. But he has this instinct for survival. So when he sees the mines are unstable, he jumps ship to the railroad valley in 1883.

He followed the infrastructure. He knew the train was the lifeline. He sets up in Kingman and initially in a small cabin, but then in 1888, he faces the enemy of every western town. Fire.

And this wasn't just a small kitchen fire.

No. Kingman in the 1880s was a tinder box. They didn't have a municipal water system yet. They were literally importing water by train.

By train. Wow.

So, when a fire started, you couldn't just turn on a hydrant. You basically stood back and hope the wind changed. Watkins lost his home and his business in that 1888 fire.

Most people would pack up. Okay. The desert tried to kill me. I'm going back east.

But Watkins, the Doner Party descendant, he just pivots.

He secures the corner of Fourth and Front Street, now Andy Divine Avenue.

And that location was strategic genius. It was the node, as the sources call it.

Absolutely. The train depot was right across the street. So if you were stepping off the train, disoriented, dusty, maybe a little sick from the journey, the first thing you saw was Watkins Pioneer Drugstore.

He positioned himself as the gatekeeper to the town.

Exactly.

So let's talk about the building he puts up because this is where that mask concept really kicks in. The sources say the original 1888 store was made of adobe,

which makes sense. Adobe is practical. It's mud. It's thick. It's cheap. And it insulates against that 110° heat better than wood.

But he didn't want it to look like adobe, did he?

No. And this is really important for understanding the psychology of the West. They didn't want to look like they were living in mud huts. They wanted to look like they were part of civilization. So Watkins dressed up this adobe box in a style called Queen Anne commercial,

which is what? Like fancy Victorian.

Think of it like putting a tuxedo on a cowboy. Yeah.

It had a decorative parapit wall to hide the flat roof. It had a recessed doorway. It was trying to project stability, permanence, and eastern sophistication.

It was a bluff. We aren't a rough frontier outpost. We are a proper Victorian town.

Precisely.

So, layer 1 is the civilized Victorian mask, but nature interferes again.

Fire. Round two. June 1907. A massive blaze guts the block.

It seems like Kingman really needed to invest in a fire department.

Eventually they did.

But this destruction, it paved the way for the second mask. They rebuild in 1908. This time they use brick. Much safer, but they also keep some of the adobe walls that survived.

So it's a composite structure.

It is, but the style changes completely. Yeah. The Victorian look is dead. Now they go mission revival.

I can picture this. This is the style with the curved Alamo looking roof lines. Right. The stuck out. Exactly. The curve linear parapets, the smooth plaster. But why? Why switch from Victorian to Spanish mission?

I assumed it was just because they were in the Southwest.

It was more calculated than that. Across the street, the Santa Fe Railroad had just built a new depot. And the railroad had a corporate branding strategy, the Romance of the Southwest.

They were selling tickets to tourists by promising them an exotic Spanish colonial adventure.

So the town was mirroring the marketing.

Precisely. was saying, "Oh, you're looking for old Spain. We have that." Even though the building was brand new and built by an Anglo pharmacist, it was branding. They were curating the view for the train passengers.

It's fascinating that the building's face is determined by who is looking at it. First, it was for the locals to feel civilized. Now, it's for the tourists on the train.

But then the observer changes again. The train starts to decline. The car takes over.

Enter Route 66. By the 1930s, Front Street is part of the Mother Road. The traffic isn't walking across the street. from the depot anymore. It's driving by at 45 mph.

And those intricate Spanish curves don't read well at that speed.

They look dated and more importantly, they look slow,

right? And in the 1930s, modern meant fast. It meant machines. So around 1935, the luy block puts on its third mask, streamline modern.

This is a style I love. It's that sleek, aerodynamic look.

It is. Think about a toaster from the 1930s or a locomotive. Horizontal lines. smooth surfaces, simple geometry. They stripped off the mission curves and squared it up.

It was an architectural faceelift to catch the eye of the motorist heading to California.

That's a great way to put it. It was survival through aesthetics. If they kept the Victorian look, they would have looked like a dusty relic. By going modern, they signaled that they were part of the future.

So, we have the exterior shifting, but I want to move inside the building because the sources talk about the Luy block as a social hub. For over a hundred years, this was a pharmacy,

Kigman Drug Company.

But in the 1920s, The function of a pharmacy changes radically in America and it's because of one specific law.

Prohibition. The Volstead Act.

Suddenly, you can't go to the saloon. It's illegal. So, where do you go to meet your friends?

You go to the drugstore. In 1920, the Luthie block underos a major remodel to install a soda fountain. And this isn't just about selling ice cream. This is a sociological shift. The drugstore becomes the third place. Not home, not work, but the community living room. It's a lot more wholesome than the saloon, I guess.

On the surface, yes, it was respectable. Men, women, teenagers could all mingle there. The sources mentioned that the building still has the original pressed metal ceilings from this era.

Oh, that's cool.

There's a detail I really like, the mezzanine windows.

I saw that windows on the second floor looking down into the store.

Right. It served two purposes. One, it let light filter through, but two, it acted as a sort of eye in the sky. The pharmacist could be up in the office doing paperwork, but they could look down through these windows to monitor the shop floor,

making sure the high school kids aren't stealing candy or getting too rowdy at the soda fountain.

A little bit of 1920 surveillance, yes, but it highlights how busy this place was. It needed management.

Now, speaking of the uh the human ecosystem of this block, there's a section in the research that I think is arguably the most interesting, but often gets overlooked.

We talk about the Anglo pioneer history a lot, but the Luthie block was actually a massive hub for Kingman's Chinese population. This is a crucial layer to uncover. In 1908, when they rebuilt and expanded the block, it wasn't just the pharmacy. They built rental bays. And one of those bays became the White House Cafe,

which sounds like a very traditional American diner.

The name was likely a strategic choice to sound familiar and safe. But it was owned and operated by the Lum family, specifically Wong Lum. And this wasn't just a brief tenency. The Lum family had been in the region since 1884.

That's almost as long as Watkins.

Exactly. And we have to Remember the context here. This is the era of the Chinese Exclusion Act. Anti-Chinese sentiment in the West was legally codified and often violent. Right?

Yet here you have a Chinese-owned business operating right next door to the town's premier pharmacy on the most prominent corner in Kingman.

It kind of disrupts the narrative of segregation we usually hear. We tend to think of Chinese immigrants in the West as being relegated to Chinatowns or railroad camps,

but here they are on Main Street. They were central to the economy. In fact, the sources note that Chinese families ran almost all the restaurants in Kingman during this period except for the corporate Harvey House.

They were feeding the miners, the ranchers, the travelers, everyone.

Yes. And there's a specific figure mentioned, Charlie Lum,

Wong Lum's nephew.

A fascinating character. He grew up in this environment, straddling two cultures. He later went on to open his own famous spot, the Jade, and became the first Chinese member of the local Elks lodge. He was is known as the pride of 66.

The pride of 66. That's a hell of a title. It is. And it shows that the Luy block wasn't just a building. It was an incubator. You had the pioneer grit of the Watkins family and the entrepreneurial resilience of the Lum family all sharing the same roof. It was a dense multicultural node in the middle of the desert.

It's amazing to think about the smells and sounds in that block in say 1925. You've got the medicinal smell of the pharmacy, the soda, fountain clinking and then right next door the sounds of a busy restaurant kitchen run by the lums.

It was the heartbeat of the town.

So fast forward, the pharmacy era is incredibly long. It lasts until the early 1990s. That is a century long run,

which is so rare. Most businesses fail in 5 years. This one lasted 10 decades.

But eventually, the Kingman drug company closes. The building has a brief identity crisis. It becomes a social club for a minute. And then in 1995, we enter the modern era. The Serrano family arrives.

And this is where the story could have ended badly. The Serrano family, who had a successful chain of Mexican restaurants, bought the building.

And in the '9s, the trend was often demolish and build new.

Exactly. They could have gutted it. They could have turned it into a Stuckco box that looked like every other Taco Bell.

But they didn't.

They chose adaptive reuse. They recognized the value of the bones. They kept the pressed tin ceilings. They kept the mezzanine. They kept the streamline modern facade, but added their own layer colorful murals, Mexican motifs.

It's like the final mask or the current mask at least

it is. They leaned into the history and by doing that they turned a restaurant into a destination

and that brings us back to the El Bandido because the food itself feels like part of this roadside attraction legacy.

It absolutely is. Route 66 has always been about the spectacle. The giant dinosaurs, the biggest ball of twine, the Serrano family created a culinary spectacle. That Christmas style burrito red and green sauce is a distinct regional marker of Arizona. A and New Mexico. It grounds the place in its geography.

And the fact that it's still a place where people gather for birthdays, where staff sing to you, where you go to cool off, it means the building is still doing the job it did in 1920.

That's the continuity. The technology changes. The menu changes from phosphates to margaritas. But the human need for a third place remains. The Luthie block survived because it never stopped serving that need.

So when we step back and look at this 140year arc, what's the big takeaway? Why should a listener in Ohio or London care about a drugstore in Kingman?

I think the lesson is about how we observe our own cities. The Luffy block teaches us that historic preservation isn't about freezing time. It's not about turning everything into a museum where you can't touch the glass.

Right. Museums are great, but you can't eat a tamalei in most of them.

Exactly. This building survived because it changed. It survived because HH Watkins didn't stubbornly stick to his adobe cabin. It survived because they covered the Victorian brick with modern stucco adaptation is the only reason it's still standing.

It's a paradox then. To preserve the building, they had to change it

consistently. So, here's my challenge to everyone listening. Next time you're walking through your downtown or driving past an old block in your neighborhood, stop and really look at the buildings.

Don't just see the coffee shop or the lawyer's office that's there now.

Right? Ask yourself, what mask is this building wearing? Is that brick facade original, or is it hiding something else? Is that trendy loft apartment Actually, an old warehouse was at Bank once, a cinema.

I love that

every building has these layers, these scars and facelifts that tell the economic story of your town. The Luy block is just one example, but they are everywhere if you learn to spot them.

It transforms a commute into a treasure hunt.

It really does.

Well, if you are ever in Kingman, go to 401 East Andy Divine Avenue. Pay your respects to the ghost of HH Watkins. Look for the traces of the White House Cafe and uh try to defeat the El Bandido.

And if you do finish the burrito, let us know. We need proof.

We'll put you on the wall of fame. Thanks for diving deep with us today. Keep looking beneath the surface. We'll catch you on the next one.

sources.title: _  El Palacio Homepage Demo Build Brief for Antigravity.pdf
sources.content:
Develop the following based on the provided Antigravity instructions: 
1. A new website wireframe. 2. A complete brand identity. 3. A set of instructions for the AI memory protocol. 
Additionally, review all source materials and provide a list of names associated with conflicting information when compared to the existing text.    El Palacio Homepage Demo Build Brief for Antigravity  This document outlines the requirements for a single-page HTML demo site for El Palacio, based on the provided wireframe and the official El Palacio Brand Identity. The focus is a high-fidelity, entirely HTML front-end representation.-----I. Project & Technical Directives 
1. Scope: Single-page El Palacio Restaurant Homepage Demo (HTML/CSS only). 2. Eliminated Features: The previous "Vision Demo" requirements for a Persona 
Switcher (Employee/Manager view) and the Interactive Proposal Tab (ROI/Roadmap) are explicitly removed. 
3. Interactivity: All interactions (e.g., link clicks, button presses) should utilize simple vanilla JS or CSS :hover states to simulate an active site experience. 
4. Aesthetic Mandate: The design must prioritize tactility and depth cues (rejecting flat minimalism) and adhere to the "Digital Luthy Block" aesthetic (stone, stained glass, Talavera tile). 
II. El Palacio Brand DNA & Visual Constraints 
Element Hex Code Descriptive Name Architectural/Aesth etic Use 
Primary #00A896 Deep Turquoise Hand-fired Weathered Talavera Tile (Interactions/Accents) 
Secondary #D95D39 Terracotta Orange Fired Clay, Earthenware (CTA Button, Secondary Accents) 
Accent #E4007D Vibrant Mexican Pink Hand-dyed Textiles (Navigation Interaction) 
Background #F8E473 Warm Cream Sun-faded Stucco (Primary background texture) 
Background Texture: All backgrounds must utilize the Warm Cream (#F8E473) hand-troweled stucco texture. Explicitly reject plain whitespace. 
Structural Borders: All structural elements and borders should reference the rustic, hand-cut Locally-Quarried Tufa Stone (e.g., thick, textured borders). 
Framing: All major content blocks (Hero, Featured Items, Testimonials, Daily Special) must use the Mission Arch Silhouette for their frames/containers. 
Voice & Tone: Content must reflect the blend of 'The Sage' (Storied, Historic) and 'The Caregiver' (Warm, Welcoming) personas, grounded in the history of the 1888 Luthy Block on Historic Route 66. 
III. Homepage Wireframe Build Guide 
Section Content & Styling Directives 
HEADER Background: Use the Deep Turquoise (#00A896) with a simulated textured brushstroke. Left: Parrot logo (primary/standing pose) linking to home. Right: Navigation links: (History | Menu | Visit). Interaction: A Pink underline (#E4007D) must appear on hover/active navigation links. 
HERO (Desktop) Frame: Full, ornate stained glass frame treatment (using all brand colors). Content (Split Layout): Left: Illustrated exterior of the actual El Palacio building (401 E Andy Devine Ave, Kingman, AZ 86401) with Spanish Revival architecture and warm sunset lighting. Right: A high-quality Food photo (e.g., The El Bandido Burrito). Top Right: Text links for "Menu" and "Our Story" inside the frame. Background: Cream (#F8E473). 
HERO (Mobile) Top/Bottom: A horizontal Talavera tile stripe (using Turquoise/Terracotta motifs). Content: Stacked layout (Building illustration first, then food image). Background: Cream (#F8E473). 
WELCOME MESSAGE Frame: No explicit frame; must float naturally on the Cream background. Content: 2-3 sentences of copy written in the Caregiver voice (focusing on family, warmth, hospitality, and allusion to the Luthy Block). Typography: Use a warm, readable Body text style. 
PARROT DIVIDER A visual divider featuring a Parrot (in-flight variation), placed between the Welcome and Features sections. 
FEATURED ITEMS Layout: 3 columns (desktop) / stacked (mobile). Item Frame: Each item must be housed in a simple Mission Arch frame. Content per item: Food photo, Item name (e.g., El Bandido Burrito, Sopapillas), and a short, one-sentence description. Background: Cream (#F8E473). 
PRIMARY CTA Background: Layered depth effect simulating a desert horizon treatment, transitioning from Cream to subtle Terracotta. Center: Large "Order Online" button with Terracotta orange textured fill (#D95D39). Parrot: A Parrot (walking variation) must be positioned to the left or right of the button. 
DAILY SPECIAL Frame: Must be contained within a Mission Arch frame. Headline: Use a playful, bold headline in the Mascot voice. Description: Short description of a special. Secondary Button: "See Full Menu" with a Deep Turquoise outline (#00A896). 
TESTIMONIALS Layout: 2-3 testimonials, side-by-side (desktop) / stacked (mobile). Item Frame: Each testimonial must be in a simple Mission Arch frame. Background: Cream (#F8E473). 
FOOTER Background: Gradient transition from Cream to Terracotta (#D95D39). Content: Clearly sectioned information: Hours, Location (including address 401 E Andy Devine Ave, Kingman, AZ 86401), Contact, and Social links. Bullets: Use a Talavera tile bullet for list items (Turquoise static → Orange on hover). Right Corner: A Parrot (sitting variation) must be included. 
-----End of Brief

sources.title: Parable of Silence, Part 10, Breath in the Breath.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence, Chapter 10:
Breath in the Breath

A very small hummingbird fluttered to me and then showed me her nest and eggs in our oak tree out front while I watered the plants. Both species have been on this property for years and trust each other. Now we know not to trim the oak this year. And a chickadee flew into our living room window but I couldn't find her. Yesterday I was sitting outside and she scampered up to me, left wing busted, asking for help. There is no one to call. So I fed her and gave her water and now she lives in the backyard. Lilly is a gentle soul, and simply sniffed her and when I told our little beagle to let her be she hasn't bothered her since. But there are cats that walk the walls at night. The chickadee hides in the wood pile.

Belief makes real, but only to an extent. When I was a junior in high school a self-proclaimed coven of witches pulled out some of my hair to find out my real age. That night I dreamt of them, and a river full of dead bodies. The next day, when one of them came up to me to tell me they knew, I told her they didn't. They never bothered me again. Another time a disgruntled employee of a health food store I was working at left a chicken head on the front doorstep. I simply threw it away and never told anyone. There have been many such instances where belief makes real psychologically, and people use this to manipulate, harm, control, or otherwise sway a person's mind, which in turn can greatly affect their body. But with this tidbit of insight, you have a great armor about you. (And of course none of these methods compare to accepting ourselves as pieces of the mystery and unknown of the universe and existence.) 

But the methods by which one may influence the minds of others, or our own minds, pales when faced with objective reality. I cannot heal the bird's wing by believing it. That takes knowledge, of which I simply do not have. 

And so we little broken tribe of sensitive dog and lame bird and sensitive and lame man enjoy the night, guarding against the feral.

When the tiny bird died I gently stroked its head in the passing. I then buried in her in the backyard. I grieved all the while.

The fact that we're not consistently in our natural state when the weather is good, drinking wine or tea or milkshakes, eating brie and strawberries, goat cheese and watermelon, curry and naan, lounging in the grass, soaking the sun, or playing and climbing trees and swimming, is entirely of our own doing. Still, we give each other awards. The fact that a bunch of mostly hairless apes, of the 5th ape variety, played dress up and it was covered as very serious by well respected news organizations, is really very funny to me. Conversely, I too like playing dress up, and there can be an art to it. And though we're mired in what some might argue is a self-destructive system and cultural construct, there are many benefits; for example, most of our lives are easier and more comfortable than even the highest royalty half a millennia ago, we have fantastic technology, which allows us access to the collective consciousness at anytime as well as its benefits of travel, entertainment, studying the world and universe (and also I would argue the reliance upon which is furthering the evolutionary trend of our shrinking brains), and for most of us our basic needs are met (though ever more not our natural, spiritual, intellectual, aesthetic and uniquely human needs - though those too, like leisure and love and music and art, can also be found elsewhere in nature, of which we are part). We have tamed ourselves. In the process, we do some awfully silly things. In some ways we've enslaved ourselves; in others we've freed ourselves. Yet we are still very much wild. Still reactive, and we still lash out like a whip come grinding when we feel trapped. Which most do. A feeling that something is not quite right. Which we blame on other people or our circumstances or our choices. There's a certain madness inherently left over after the taming of a wild thing.

We’ve established in this experiment over the past year that our consciousness is one scale of a universal fractal. And like the rest of the fractal, it is so both by ever solving the questions it itself posits, and then putting that solution back into the equation, over and again, as we are aware of being aware, an infinite feedback loop that when graphed on a complex plane creates endless repeating self-similar patterns, all the way up, and all the way down, but also as nodes or filaments or fingers within the fractal as the sensory organs of that for which we can neither comprehend nor have words for even if we did. Interestingly, when you turn that fractal sideways, viewing it laterally, you see another fractal, this one of time and choices creating continually doubling branches of and through time. Simultaneously, every choice, through its actualization, collapses every other choice not made. But these choices never made do not cease to exist, but live on as a kind of phantom limb. And we sometimes call this regret, and this is one of the ways we prove our subjective free will, because we believe we could have chosen differently. In either case, that sideways view of fractals is time, it is movement through duration, both of which are encompassed in our concept of time. It’s fractals in every direction, and direction itself. And time and consciousness are coterminous. They share the same physical and conceptual space. And just like our evolution advancing through leaving or being exiled from duration proper through the conceptualization of time into past, present, and future, so that we could simulate the future and survive, so are the very limits of our thoughts themselves created by our auditing and editing time. Thoughts take time. And that time is the boundaries of our thoughts. In that simulation was repetition. Repetition creates patterns creates meaning. Thus we became one of the most successful pattern recognizing minds on the planet. And by perceiving and applying patterns in and onto reality and experiencing meaning through an exchange of information and free energy, we create order from the disorder of time’s arrow, that is, entropy. Consciousness and life itself is necessarily negentropic, or anti-entropic. We utilize time to fight time. 

Just as everything is expanding infinitely faster into the source from which it came, such that, like the source or singularity, the nothing into which it expands into is potentially everything, or pure potential, as the undifferentiated unity itself was defined by the absence of everything else, and is therefore a self-contained but open and expanding system, so too is human consciousness as a mirror and reflection of the universe, prone and subject to the same forces and laws of this universe, working in the same way. Therefore, when I am up at three in the morning trying to measure my own mind as infinitesimally and in as detailed a way as possible, where time stretches off into eternity relative to my perception, where at some point I am witnessing my own self-generating out of pure potential, I am expanding the order and information of the universe and creating patterns of myself, ever recorded as information, belonging to the larger patterns of time and space and gravity and quarks and leptons and axioms of choice and all the rest.

This is an important conceptualist analogy for understanding that the universe may in fact eventuate into that which is very much alive.

The last two nights I have barely slept, if at all, wondering how to heal the mind's attendance to the wolves of words, voices so loud and menacing, implacable and thoroughly strewn throughout I can't cover myself with the dark, pitchless silence and idyll gyre into being, remaining ignorant of where birds end their cacophony and go to sleep at night.

Then comes morning. It is a great pleasure, after having planted and cared for a garden, to witness a little economy of flits of consciousness, as if an intake of breath grabs the attention, then surprisingly sounds its élan vital, crawling and fluttering and worming about, chemical signals with each breath of wind and water and light, roots meandering to ground the theater of life. Even those problem solvers who have their own city above our own, whom we rarely think about, dive down into the foray, making homes of feathers, needles, spit, twigs and bone, helping to flesh out the secret biome. A little swelling paradise in our backyard.

My wife’s family are all extremely hard working and not so sensitive on the face of things. Every weekend there are manual projects around our house, or a family member’s house, that not only need addressing, but there problems need to be solved. Needless to say, this family is intelligent, mathematically inclined, have giant hearts, take care of each other, and are resourceful and volitionally (as in, “if I have nothing to do I’m going to fix something”) labor intensive and physically and recreationally active. For myself it’s been a gift of the utmost treasure of meaning to be part of it. But it took a long time to adjust. I have worked and labored very hard, and I’ve the back and hands to prove it. But I also require time to think, to read, to write for extended periods. And so I adapted, and now do most of my “work” late at night or early in the morning. If we both had not adjusted slightly in some cases, and sharply in others, we probably would not have remained mated partners for so long. And if we hadn’t or couldn’t, and also that we actually have, is how those historical familial survival personalities and strategies survive.

Still, there are 8 billion people on the planet. Certainly each one of us add or not to that survival, by ourselves surviving and attempting to flourish, but these meta-strategies, when traced back, bottleneck, as in, at one time - about 900,000-years-ago, and again about 70,000-years-ago, and more times that we do not know of - the entire hominin and human population were counted at just approximately a little more than 1,200, due either to disease or natural phenomena or internecine warring or so many other factors. Or all of them. It’s frankly quite miraculous to have come to the gate and just stepped out of it at all.

Unlike conceptually closed systems and their transcendence through awareness, we would fool ourselves with either the presumption or belief, or hope even that this works the same with thoughts of other living beings, in or out of their presence. That requires actions. That requires words. That requires choices. Their presence in and out of our minds are truer than any of our judgments or presumptions.

What is known is that we are now in the Anthropocene, where humanity not only supplies the destinity for humankind but for nature as well as the other species residing therein. The path chosen means an end to many species and maybe most of us, and is certainly detrimental to the planet.

Ironically this is also due in part to what Kierkegaard lamented as another project of modernity, the externalized and collectivized movement toward a mass society and the end of the individual. This unquietude, this determination to the Law of Large Numbers has been commented on as the avarice of modernity by the likes of Jaspers, Ortega, Berdyaev, Marcel and more - but ultimately we find the source in Kierkegaard's "subjective truth". 

In Kierkegaard we find the aesthete, or person of the senses and pleasure. Next to evolve is the ethical person, who lives by universal maxims. Finally (and each is encompassed in the other as a centrifugal force) we encounter the ethico-religious person, who chooses themselves again and again and for a lifetime in existence, seeing themself as they are in their involvement, rather than in the detachment of thought. 

It is choice which brings them out of the purely ethical and its contradictory maxims and situations where maxims don't make the cut, where one has to choose between a good and a good and thus an evil. The choice is not a recondite universal, but a choice of oneself completely in all their aspects. It is an acceptance as well. 

Nietzsche never quite accepted his ugly, infirm side. Zarathustra tells the dwarf on his back and his sibilant declarations of ineptitude, either you or I! When Zarathustra commands the herder to bite off the head of the venemous snake that is trying to enter into his mouth, the herder does so, spitting out the head and laughing madly. But Zarathustra should have said to the dwarf, You and I; he should have instructed the herder to swallow the snake and thus accept his ugly unconsciousness. 

In order to choose ourselves completely, to be as we are, to be that involved, we must accept every part of ourselves. We not only choose humanity when we choose ourselves, but the universe itself.

And this ultimate responsibility to be played with lightly, we creative animals? This joy that is life? This impetus that makes taking our own lives so hard? This endlessly complex horrific beauty?

The children have always sensed this.

Every secret thought and wish and dread of ours is not only recorded, but known by us even as the universe knows itself. We keep secrets from everybody. We keep secrets from ourselves. And yet not all secrets are shameful or harmful or silly. Some secrets have a kind of power of identity. But all are vulnerable cradling, even as we were once cradled. And they are vulnerable to the interrogations of others’ perceptions.

This is one of my secrets. When I was around the age of five, I was allowed to roam the painted desert freely and all day. On one such adventure I came upon corralled wild horses. Over time I befriended one of them, and fed her carrots, which was the only thing growing at home at that time. I called her Star for the diamond on her forehead. I must have visited her for months.

One day I brought my friends with me, and they dared me to go inside the corral. As I walked into the midst of that harem, they backed away and surrounded and circled me. And then, suddenly a giant black horse charged. I heard him before I saw him, and turned around just as his front knees collided with my chest and knocked me flat on my back. He continued rearing up and I knew I would be trampled. Stamped out of existence.

And then perhaps more surprisingly, Star came from his left side so fast and so hard, ramming him with the such force, that both horses flew over me. A white horse watched it all outside of the circle.

I got up, still not having caught my breath, and backed up slowly toward the fence, and crawled through.

When I was five my life was saved by a horse.

There is no awareness without attention, and no attention without choice, whether our own or by our instincts or bodies or the choices of others. Such is cause and effect an awareness through choices within which and from which we perceive time. Cognitive time is an innovation that comes closer to fitting reality than any of our other tools. Time is so integral to reality, so real, we are confronted with it not only in everything we will ever be conscious of, but also in how and why we are conscious. Fortunately, we do not know it in its entirety. Or perhaps ever. It also encompasses the primary conceptual example of the unknown. The infinite. Eternity. Relativity. The time before time is thus very hard to conceptualize. It is only because of this conceptual perception of time that we mentally move both backward by being aware of the history of our awareness, which is also counterintuitively moving forward, as we must, with the arrow of time. This is how we paused and continue to pause time. This is focusing so intently that the measurement and the time that takes approaches infinity. By touching the ephemeral and protean space between conceptualizing eternity and also and contrarily focusing so minutely on a millisecond that that contradiction separated and disconnected and detached in its creation our more expansive minds from the all encompassing presence of our bodies, we created a pocket within which we expand. Part and parcel of the ever expanding fundamental forces of existence.

We creatures can sense awareness in one another. If we keep our awareness open we realize that the other animals speak too. With their mantles or songs or growls or chuffs. But more than that, they speak with their breath. 

Before language was a thought, consciousness rises like a maw out of water, self expression out of reflection. And in that breath is a breath. There lies an immediacy that returns us to duration. There resides, sometimes, beauty and gentleness beyond imagining. We can have that with each other as well. We can have that with ourselves.

sources.title: Parables of Silence A Thought Experiment - 1.PNG
Parables of Silence, Afterward, Time Sculptors .txt
sources.content:
Afterward: Time Sculptors
“In music the passions enjoy themselves.”
Nietzsche, Beyond Good and Evil, 106
The angle of the polarity that charges oxygen and hydrogen negatively and positively and compels both through attraction and repulsion their marriage and bond, which contains the property of wetness, which neither oxygen nor hydrogen atoms possess on their own, is as the emergent property of consciousness from arranging time and matter in specific and highly complex patterns, where time is the motion and rotation of the angle of polarity, and matter the rest.
Emergence, though as a whole containing properties that its constituents and individual components do not, is a kind of loop, a kind of translation of time and matter, just as emotions are translated by consciousness from the body, just as the body translates the choices and states of consciousness.
Another feedback loop is fractals, when, for instance, in the most famous example, the Mandelbrot Set, a very simple complex number is put into and solved in an equation, and then that more complex solution is put back into the same equation, and the process continues indefinitely, as long as the loop does not exceed the number 2 or more, which would release and race out into infinity, but instead the equation is captured in a system that looks like infinitely repeating patterns on every scale possible.
Snowflakes, the fjords of Norway, coastlines, ferns, non-linear decision trees, the populations of some animals, conch shells, the double helix, neural networks, eyes of hurricanes, the spiral of the Andromeda galaxy, the web of the universe. The geometry of the fractal is such that its repetition at every scales can be zoomed in or out infinitely.
Interestingly, when you turn that fractal sideways, you encounter period doubling bifurcations. The system bifurcates because it is made out of, in, and as time. It is movement through duration. And then it doubles again and again until it reaches a chaotic state. This pattern can be observed in dripping faucets, the rate at which populations grown and shrink, and much more. But also in our choices, where the decision hinges on two options, which has four variables, which have eight potential consequences, and on and on, until the system becomes random to a point that it must renormalize into a more complex and more ordered and aware system. Usually this is when we are confronted with, or become aware of,  the innate incompletenesses or gaps or uncertainties or contradictions - all of these push consciousness to a more expansive and highly ordered awareness. This renormalization is due to our inherent need, which conjoins being aware, to turn our mind’s eye on itself and to measure our own minds, much less existence, from inside of our own minds.
Every system of complex thought is either inconsistent or incomplete. Mathematics, physics, biology, philosophy, poetry, economics, politics and religion and more. You cannot prove the truth of a system by using its own rules. You need an outside perspective.
And so awareness loops back to and offers itself and feeds therefrom recursively and mostly through attention and intention, which are constitutive of choices, feeds its solutions back into the equation, constantly, as the mind’s eye looks back at itself reflected in the last mind’s eye watching, and that too is being watched, and so on, and this is how we scission time into the limits and shapes and boundaries of our thought patterns. And this is why we are always solving reality and remembering, in order to better predict the future and inform our choices. We invented the future in order to survive.
This is why we became aware of the possibility of other dimensions and pockets and angles, relative to the observer, of time outside of duration proper.
This is why we utilize time as a tool and the very scaffolding of thought. In our escape we were exiled, but in that break we truly began to fight the thermodynamic arrow and direction and undoing pointing toward the death of the universe. Because we are thus open but self-contained. Boundless. Exchanging our being with our environment. Growing, as in both growing up and expanding.
———————
After laying the foundations, the thought experiment takes off following the idea that consciousness is a natural phenomenon and emergent property of the universe. At first glance that seems like calling water wet. But what makes up water? A combination of two gases that, when combined properly, creates wetness, neither of which on their own contain the property of wetness.
Consciousness itself, under these suppositions, would be counter to subjective dualism or the mind as the special seat at the head of the soul, which is normally understood as being distinct from the body or this experience of reality. Here consciousness is inseparable from this universe, and as such from the laws and forces that then apply to it.
Once we have lightly accepted that consciousness is born out of this universe, we can apply those same rules of existence, as we understand them, to our minds.
We do this by being skeptically honest and logical, while also knowing it is a game without requisite winners, seriously playing with concepts that might normally frighten us out of our reveries.
To do this we posit a consciousness that in order to know itself created a principal and prototypical pattern by creating infinitely many specific forms, objects and beings, which are both itself and not itself, in order to reflect to and on itself to itself.
This is also represented on a microscopic scale in our own consciousnesses.
This binary tension that existence itself manifests throughout is that prime pattern, the tensity of which is an endlessly elastic friction and agitation and the birthplace of consciousness, as that separation from the body, while always being connected thereto.
And as the forms of what exists are defined by their constraints and opposites and limits, silence defines substance. These are also the shapes of the patterns of our thoughts, which our intention and attention and awareness cut and sculpt out of time, an awareness that can be pinpoint or span the cosmos, as we understand it.
We are not passively within space and time, eroded slowly by their river. We actively use them as the structure and content of consciousness.
Because our minds are made of the universe, we cannot see our own minds completely, since every system that is demonstrably true cannot prove itself using its own axioms, but needs a reference point outside of itself. This is why existence outside of ourselves is both seemingly truer, and, how our minds reflect the universe back to the universe itself.
In the way that there is slightly more matter than antimatter, so the error in the system, the wobble in the equation, allows for life and the awareness of life, keeping the system open. Perfection would be the period at the end of our story.
Through generational trauma and the disassociation of a prey species, we found the future, and knowledge of our freedom in the past, a freedom and will that we then could apply not only to the present but simulations of that future. The subjective proof of our free will is analogous to the fact that most people do not know what they are talking about, but it’s the assumption that they do that allows them to speak in the first place. Or, and perhaps more appropriately, it’s analogous to the fact that meaning from relationships is total and even consuming, especially romantic, but there are millions of possible relationships that could have entangled, but whose possibilities collapsed through choice and immediacy and feeling and circumstance and geography. But the totality of those possible meanings and their hunger and satisfactions, would have been, though different and particular and specific, just as all encompassing.
As the spiral of the Milky Way is reflected in the eye of a hurricane here on Earth, and those are reflected in a nautilus shell, which is reflected in DNA, and so on, on the scale in which we are normally aware consciousness is a sense organ of the universe, just as it is a sense organ of and for itself and the world. This reception of reality means that we are not only nerve endings, and eyes and ears and music, and feelings too, but the frontal lobes of existence. And we watch our internal awareness with the same awareness of the patterns of reality.
These operational truths do not quite touch the mystery of reality, for the aforementioned reasons about open systems and incompleteness, and yet we physically adapt to our own illusions. This is not entirely a bad thing, since ignorance births the impetus to know more, to lay down the road even as we drive upon it.
All in all, this thought experiment does not take away from our specialness, uncomfortably and reasonably apparent in all of our myths, but rather reframes our place in this universe as the universe itself, as existence itself. Here may you find that you are home.
We are not strangers here.
———————
“We have fixed up a world for ourselves in which we can live - assuming bodies, lines, planes, causes and effects, motion and rest, form and content: without these articles of faith, nobody now would endure life. But that does not mean that they have been proved. Life is no argument; the conditions of life could include error.”
Friedrich Nietzsche, The Gay Science, 121
Let’s imagine as a tailpiece and postlude and proxy thought experiment, a person who was incrementally figuring out reality, piece by piece, and as they did, not only did coincidences happen more often, but the experience of the world became more preternaturally and inexorably inexplicable and fantastic, as in white orbs floating around or colored lines following people or hearing other people’s thoughts through outside mediums. Now imagine that the final answer and solution, the understanding of all was achieved, that the person figured out the ultimate secret of reality.
We can postulate that the person in question would then be called mentally ill, for consciousness is in this way accepted by society as that which may never know reality completely, and the person then would probably have very odd behavior, contradictory to normative rules and society.
We can speculate on this because after having figured out existence itself, that person would necessarily come back to and still be just like themselves.
This is also the problem with the will. It is an assumption that the will or willing is an intensity and focus of the mind. But as suggested previously, we feel with every thought. This can be best explained by techniques and the strategies of predictive and penetrating inspective searching that consciousness employs but that willing does not naturally achieve. Possibly, willing unfurls from the emotions, even if its fire was sparked by the mind proper. And like all emotions, they lose steam, dwindle and are replaced in a vanishing act we don’t normally clock. Our wills implode or fizzle because they try to generate infinite energy from themselves, and this exclusion of new energy and information closes their systems, which leads to decay. The will is the antithesis of intention and attention and awareness, which sculpts the scaffolding of our thoughts, which are so much like the world because they are the world, through creating limits, outside of which we are ignorant, an ignorance that is the fuel for curiosity, and that leads to learning and creativity, which only happens in an open system.
But the mind will still pretend that it is willing, trying to cast up that intensity again and again, only to either through that illusion become delusional against the world, or the world against the mind, or both, a kind of spiritual warfare, or, believe through its pretend willing that it has figured out reality, which is the basis of delusions.
On the outside this feels and looks like someone who can’t let go of the previous moment of awareness. The awareness of being aware, which transitions each momentary scissioning and creative play in time and thought, is halted, like dogmatic beliefs in the absolutes of religion or science or politics or the rest, and therein the arrested awareness, which internally keeps trying to draw up another awareness, to instinctually keep becoming, ceases its evolution and closes, becomes entropic and disordered, and decays. It only accomplishes this waning by another kind of emotion that is conjoined with awareness, and that is panic. A small animal cornered and frozen in terror.
Consciousness is not a luxury, but a dynamic and intentional phenomenon. It was not born from comfort, but trauma responses and empathy over innumerable generations. Most of very person does not know what they’re taking about, but the assumption that they do allows them to speak in the first place. We love to know. It becomes as a part of us. But learning is not about being smart, for we know so little, so preening those feathers is an ostensibly absurd performance. Learning is about survival, and consequently, the survival of life and consciousness in the universe. By identifying ourselves solely with what we presume we know, we risk obliteration again and again.
Much of the quotidian psychological insights work mainly because we largely live in safe environs of plenty, albeit with labor and consumer oriented stress, along with interpersonal induced stress as well as trauma. For example, the paranoia of a man whose house has previously been bombed and has then been forced to move and now believes that at anytime his new house could be bombed is rather a fine-tuned projection of fear than a guilt-based one. And the depression of a starving child is a rather exact response to the circumstances than simply a chemical imbalance. And certainly diagnostics vanish in the face of the very real horrors experienced across the planet, both internal and external. This doesn't make the agony experienced by those who are in mental distress in post-industrialized societies any less real, imperative, existent and profoundly consuming.
That being said, it is often the case that that which one resists, if not then being created by, then reifies what is being resisted, and worse, it infects, and the resister becomes in various ways that which is being resisted, along with the accompanying emotion, which is usually fear. My seat at the head is old and weary, where I've dined on my soul for far too long. Funny how the simple things keep us from being apes without apparent cages (though cages there be in ever expanding circles), and thus begin to feed on ourselves, needing myths and other already trodden mental pathways and stories to find our way out of our own cages and journey this brief existence. We live by stories, those we tell ourselves, those that help us, those we learn to think by, and those we tell of ourselves, to ourselves. Words are the incantations by which we create reality through definitions and thus meaning, and this is at once one of the gifts and burdens of being sensors of the universe, the nerve endings of that for which there is no language and which is beyond comprehension.
Just as popular art reflects public consciousness, there's a profound difference between the ontological regards of a 13th c. Madonna with Child over votive candles vs. a Giacometti thin man of the early 20th c. There's a very large distinction between the 14th c. interpretation of humanism channeled through paint-stripped classical Greek statues and the Matrix or Terminator. This early morning I was listening to some music and was wondering about the differences in syllables with regard to thought processes between contemporary simple repetitions like the Amen Break and say Bach’s canons or much older meters with seemingly chaotic syncopation reflected in other parts of the world still. I was wondering about these reflections of consciousness and their influence on its reflex-loop concerning a regard toward one's own being in the world and thought patterns and habitual conceptual structures. Repetition creates patterns creates meaning.
Observation is interactive and relational and recursive and open. It is not like a stranger watching you in public so attentively and for so long that you physically shrink and begin making uncontrollable facial expressions that themselves are trying to convey a mix of annoyance and anger and fear and angst and disgust. And if we know the attentor intimately enough, we feel the pull of their affection instead, perhaps a slight smile on our lips and with clear eyes we turn to them. But all of the above is also the universe witnessing itself.
No. Your own mind’s witnessing itself and the world is precisely and also the perception of the universe itself, beginning to know itself.
In its modest and beautiful and playful forms, the security and leisure that allows for thinking and art is itself the limits, and engenders over and again the boxes we so often aforementioned.
A mixed martial artist could not defeat a tiger in hand to paw combat, because that fighter is exiled from duration by a mind that paused time thousands and thousands of generations ago to more deeply perceive. In their strategy they are always catching up, and when our bodies seem to move miraculously, or seem to be moves ahead in the future, we discern the movement as a fulfillment of predictive ability beyond our mental awareness, an almost magical calculation in time, and we sometimes call this luck, or being in the zone, or the benefits of training, and the reason why we train our bodies with katas or yoga or boxing or running or holding our breaths or enduring extreme temperatures or anything else we discipline our bodies into reflex or out of reflex.
Still, there are brief moments of presence, of sunlight and breeze and love and comfort, which all of our species’ embodiments and ancestors knew too, and too few of, and this inherent meaning is reflected in the very essence and processes of what we create and value above all else. It was all worth it, this life.
We apply and perceive patterns to and on reality, even if we have to, in that ordering disorder, move and manipulate space and gravity (and time) to survive, as in the Innsbruck experiment.
Fortunately, we can always update this hallucination to come closer and closer to an approximation and fidelity of and to reality, ever more intimate but never touching, and thus the system remains open to the unknown.
In the growth within an infinite vacuum, our limits provide the near-breaking tension that provides the limits and boundaries and temporal frameworks that engender the tone and resonance and music that allows for thought itself. And those limits are also our biases, or cultural conditioning, who we’ve read, our memories, with whom we have conversed and those conversations, who we’ve loved and feared, our dreams, our traumas, even our compassion and affection and sometimes loathing for ourselves.
It has been a series of stress tests from the beginning for our species, and all the species we’ve been. These introduce contradictions and periods of chaos that some nobly fall to, and others transcend those limits and perceive a higher order of information and ability to take in and create more complex patterns of that information and meaning, and the system remains open and alive. When we learn, we do this internally and at the speed of our choice.
The antagonist of this story has been entropy as time’s arrow. But we’ve discussed that we perceive different dimensions of time. The past and future and infinite possibilities. Time as the limits of consciousness can also therefore be an open system. We use time to fight time. And we can do this because time and consciousness share the same conceptual boundaries. We have so much more to see. So many more dimensions to witness, and thus think. Time is not only devolving the universe and all life back to the source and obliterating reality, but also it has the potential to unfold as information and awareness infinitely in all directions. Our maturity is so far ahead it is not only beyond our sight but beyond our ken, just as the fact that we do not know we are the universe is one of the mechanisms by which the universe knows itself. But the awareness of ourselves as such does not diminish that mechanism or experience, on both counts, on all counts, but like all expansions of awareness, adds depth and meaning, as when we learn that honey bees see targets on some flowers in ultraviolet to guide them, and this adds depth and layers to our own perception of bees and flowers and the interrelationship of every specific thing.
The limits and shapes of thoughts through their borders allow for the acoustics of thoughts and their patterns, their music, their reflective flowing and fluctuations born from zero point foam, as water foams when its surface tension is reduced by the introduction of another fluid dynamic like the air we need to breathe, these abstractions birthed from eternity without knowing why or what it is in its entirety, its source hiding so that is can be found, but never completely, thus fueling the drive to know itself, its recorded echo bouncing infinitely off the crags of existence and their resonance, their overlays and undertones and sounds and colors and textures, which are the synesthesia of time. As we create through awareness and perception and attention and intention time functionally as the scaffolding of thought, as well as through consciousness peering into and thereby birthing novel dimensions in and out of time and therefrom new dimensions of information and order. Again, there are untold dimensions to be perceived. Even our newer mental observations in some hypotheses and comic book and science fiction movies, are suspect as prattle and chubby fingers clenching a rattle, watching the quiver of a top trying for symmetry and dancing across the surface of time.
A raven hovers over the forest of Mingus Mountain while a man pretends, plays and posits his psyche on the creature, both beginning the dive toward cliffside Jerome. Did our cousins know this dexterity, witness reflective gestures, floresiensis, neanderthalensis, denisovans, erectus and sapiens and more walking the earth at the same time?
A becoming intrinsic to curiosity and fun what chirps, calls, signs and songs refined feelings further, looking upwards for a refresher course on space-time, adding meaning with each respire of larger brains, information layered in fractal recursion, trees to breathing, marshlands hiding in the liver, nothing completely aleatory, a universe filled with mystery and reasons, Buber’s I-Thou sweeping throughout?
How to heal an ear’s attendance with each focus and rumination, how to scry during every communion?
We are still learning that whether or not we have souls, we feel soulfully. We are still learning to accept the minds of other animals, even consciousness in ourselves.
Where I live in the desert when it rains the little yellow flowers of the creosote bush perfume the air and mix with wet soil waiting to burst with life. Our puppy Lilly plays outside and chases shades and ghosts of drops as if there were no tools or time to set them free, and I think about our cousins and how we leave them covered in neglect and forget the family that surrounds us still. Inside cumin has married the pinto beans and breakfast is almost ready, and I call Lilly with the tenderness afforded my own cherished sense of self.
Let’s remember, that as measurement approaches infinity, not only is the ruler necessarily infinitesimally focused, but that the time it takes to measure itself approaches infinity, such that the closer one regards the scales of reality or consciousness, the more time it takes to perceive that information. A man in a plane surveying the Cliffs of Dover takes less than an hour to do so, whereas an atomic force microscope may take eternity. In this way human consciousness focused so sharply and intently that it was able to hold a single moment, which was eternity long, and in that contradiction of time’s perception thus transcend and essentially pause time and step back into itself and into new dimensions of time, like past and present and future, where boundaries were sculpted by attention and intention out of time. We are just beginning to perceive all of the forms of multiplicities of time’s possibilities within and as the universe. These too are still baby steps.
In the becoming of the other, through our own participant observations of our own becoming, like all mimicry in the collective, we felt that awareness of being aware before we were ourselves conscious to it, and therefore it, of it, as with all translations of scales of awareness. And so that birth of awareness transferred, not quite like an infection, but a benevolent contagion, from translating the feeling of witnessing another aware, through intimate ontological mimicry, to others of our species. This mental empathy took generations to sublimate the dendritic light and spark that created a feedback loop so that our brains dramatically grew and complexified for the whole of our species leading itself to more awareness. This loop is not ambagious, but expanding., as with individual consciousness, where the open fractal loops are constitutively the solved answers of questions being fed back into the questions, which generate more question and their solutions. The mathematics of our brains are always solving reality and the reality of our minds.
This spread and transfer and translations of consciousness and intelligence, through the active ingredient that is time, through learning itself, is also the universe seeking to know itself, or so we have posited in this thought experiment, although we wouldn’t be offended were it pointed out that we were here projecting our own anthropomorphism, concepts all of which we do not completely nor perhaps will ever fully understand, as lions would worship lions if they were able.
Your paying attention is actively and actually fighting for life and against the death of all life and this universe it resides in, in the exact sense, while concurrently the given reason and weapon is awareness itself, and consciousness itself.
Here may be a way that not only consciousness may use less energy by knowing and organizing being conscious of more information, without compression, as in the functional and explanatory heavy lifting and shortcuts of patterns, like a Go master verses the novice who must find every path available of every piece and their relationships to each other, and also create order out of disorder ever more expansively and thoroughly, and so illuminate on and change the course of the coming dark.

sources.title: Parables of Silence, Part 1, The Wolves of Words.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence, Chapter 1: The Wolves of Words
The truth, said Taleswapper, when we see it, is ridiculous, and if we wish to worship it, we must never allow ourselves to see it.
Orson Scott Card
Water is my eye, most faithful mirror.
Massive Attack, Teardrop
There’s something quieter than sleep Within this inner room! It wears a sprig upon its breast, And will not tell its name.
Emily Dickinson
Quis videt?
1
Paracelsus
Given that most likely Homo sapiens and hominins, for at least the past 300,000 years, more likely up to 8 million years and perhaps beyond, have had a sense of spirituality, having believed in spirits and gods before there were words for such things, it might behoove us to take the concept seriously, though we’ve known since at least Kant’s Antinomies that these can neither be proven nor disproved, insofar as reasoning itself creates contradictory logical propositions, for and against, each of which are justified and can both be confirmed and confuted by experience itself, and posit as a thought experiment what God might look like as a fact.
Perhaps some of the best sources we can utilize for our mythology is Jakob Böhme’s creation story and thus Christian mysticism, as well as Advaita Vedanta and Sufism and more. In fact, most of the mystic and spiritual traditions lead to the notion of universal consciousness, before the inevitable error of introducing myths as facts. We will recursively revisit this mythology as the ground and scaffolding of this thought experiment.
2
God and I are like two giant fat people sitting in a tiny boat, we just keep bumping into each other and laughing.
Hafiz
God might then be an undifferentiated unity defined by the absence of everything else, that is, the Source, or Abyss.
Creation would then be the result of the Source dividing infinitely from its state of original unity, and therefore, God is not omniscient nor omnipotent, since it is eternal and unique. It knows no beginning, and also nothing like itself, and also no end. And if it is the case that God created humanity in its own image, it did so that it could learn about itself.
God creates dichotomies and opposites. Such are the beginnings and catalysts of individuation. The tension between God’s positive and negative aspects boils down to an exigential need to know itself and cosmic loneliness. The positive force is the part of It that chose to differentiate itself in search of self-knowledge; the negative force is the part of It that seeks to return to its original unified state, thus obliterating reality in the process. The negative force is the divine angst, the eternal frustration of seeking a goal that can never be accomplished again, that perfect unity before the eating of the tree.
All of this is Its creation of Its natural reaction to being the only consciousness which is not conscious of a world, for It is the world of which It is conscious. But the means by which it knows itself, the life born out of the universe’s (which is It) natural processes and emerging consciousnesses, are faced with the same mysteries (the mystery of itself) which inspired Its need to know Itself in the first place.
God would then be a dichotomous, fractal, self-repeating and self-generating extension of information, while the universe’s phase space eventuates into genetic domains, all resulting from the existential tension created by Its desire for self-knowledge.
God, in essence, is consciousness, reflected in every individual differentiated even as they reflect on themselves, for they embody the qualities of their source, self-reflection in eternal movement, ever putting together the how without a concern for why, kinematic, a movement whose why is lost in translation because it is unimportant to the moved and mover that necessitates space and time as the structure of the imaginings and of all forms of consciousness, as the structure of the universe, a consciousness reflecting on itself, mind strewn throughout rudimentarily in plants and bugs and every life, the content and context of which is information, seeking to know itself, ever becoming, all the while striving to return to the source and thus escape and nullify reality.
God is the universe, which is consciousness.
3
“It is really quite impossible to say anything with absolute precision, unless that thing is so abstracted from the real world as to not represent any real thing.”
Richard Feynman
Its only answer that external reality could explain to itself outside of itself about itself within the dissolution and becoming in which the self recognizes itself as conscious, is that consciousness can and will project its unconsciousness upon the world, past an intuitive disinterestedness of reality. Consciousness, like anything else, is relative in space-time, a pulsing of unmaking, unmasking, making and masking within the duration of wider time. Always becoming or unraveling, a dilatant state is never achieved.
The world sometimes speaks to us of us through the language and conversations or other mediums of strangers, or electronics or even the wind. Our unconsciousness projects our innermost selves onto the world and through others to reflect ourselves back to us. The space-time of the collective consciousness supports the space-time reality of individual consciousness - a listening occurring where the self stumbles into a larger arena within a more distant horizon that tells the self of itself, vaguely understood and compartmentalized as unconscious structure. Here is where we find Jung’s shadows at play. Here are the words that we fear and that would destroy us.
And there are varying manifestations of the gravity of thought: Hiroshima, government, Christ, discipline, poetry, crack, the words having had enough affect in their recurrence to be ready fill-ins in consciousness; and expectations - how we expect our perception of reality draws in weight of mass and joules of energy like planets - but we could never control the expectations of our perceptions completely, and perhaps not much at all, never ever or ever having been made or having become.
Thoughts are not islands in a river, as James wrote. There is the apex of ripples and waves and the dip between and quivering light and the cool depth of the water and the night sky above. The stream of consciousness does not solidify and is almost diaphanous when viewed in the universal river that contains it. There is no mud bank for separate safety, but substance nothing without the rest, illumined most in the mix.
4
“All the choir of heaven and furniture of earth - in a word, all those bodies which compose the mighty frame of the world - have not any subsistence without a mind.”
Bishop Berkeley
For all of the above, consciousness is currently unexplained, but the consensus is that matter arranged in specific ways engenders the emergent phenomena of awareness, self-reflection and sentience. Some think that quantum processes are at its core, including entanglement and super-position, but this too would not explain the vast, endless possibilities of the mind. For example, it seems to be that the intentionality of minds, either instinctual or willed or playfully imagined, can create endless forms in and out of the Source, and the Source as well, as evidenced by you reading this sentence, including the content and context and information within and making up those forms, which seem to be inherently mathematical in nature.
It is possible that the recurrent, universal nature of language is analogous, and that the way we know for certain that there are other minds is not only through the mirrors of others, or through our empathetic and deep soulful feelings, but also those very geometric and rational patterns of consciousness.
Perhaps Plato’s Ideas and Forms then are those patterns that both exist as recurring in space-time in the universe, as well as those that repeat in space-time within language (or language as such) and life itself. Perhaps Plato’s Forms are the patterns within the collective consciousness, which is analogous to the extension and mirror of the universe.
5
Assumption 1: Consciousness belongs to, and is a part of, this universe. It is a natural phenomenon.
Assumption 2: The nature of the universe and its creations is patterns, which recur in space and time, and this is the same for consciousness.
Assumption 3: Existence itself is impetus, movement fighting against inertia, and this impelling energy manifests as patterns, and it is the same with consciousness.
Assumption 4: There would be no movement of patterns without space and time, and space-time is fundamental to the structure of both the universe and consciousness.
Assumption 5: The physical laws of reality and the universe are mirrored in the workings of individual consciousness, from its very structure to the scaffolding used to arrangement of concepts and ideas and to its illusory nature used for its very survival. Our minds are perfect reflections of the universe, though our information be little, microscopic in fact, comparatively.
6
“In my beginning is my end.”
T. S. Eliot, East Cocker
One must please forgive the circular and stream-of-conscious nature of section 3. Our minds can sniff out patterns like none else that we know of, which are made of repetitions, and those patterns create meaning. Repetition creates patterns creates meaning, all of which are that which recurs in space and time. Often in our mental rigor we both widen the circle of reasoning from particulars for the whole story, then focus in again. For all conversations are circular, but what seemingly matters is the circumference. Part 3 was a way to show how both work together in a given mental state. The information, the content, is valid, but it would have been more straight forward to say that outside of our individual minds, the world and universe defines us, through reflections of ourselves, as our minds are reflections of unified consciousness, the universe ever-expanding into the unknown, that impossible concept beyond the self-containment of the extension, needing to know itself, as our minds, our imagining and thinking extends and reaches and forms patterns along the way toward understanding, which has no voice over reason.
Each moment the movement of consciousness makes information out of seeming nothingness, a Big Bang forever replaying in its flux creating existence itself from nothing. What potential. What this movement and impulse is to the very structure of consciousness, so out of the very making and being as fact of our consciousness does space-time serve the same functions for our universe as well as its reflections and need to know itself, that is, our minds. Such are these forces inherent in the being of consciousness as its structure within and upon which all movement and volition evolve that their presence is rarely noticed in the mental play. The content is the focused upon. The coalescence of matter and information and ideas happens as naturally as the nature of the universe itself.
Molecules and modes of thought begin to bundle together, connect, sometimes in endosymbiosis, to both absorb and dispense entropic thermal energy more thoroughly and effectively, and protect themselves against the ravages of extinction and survive. And the universe evolves. And life evolves from mere matter. So do ideas string together and the circle widens for an understanding to release the tension of encountering new incomprehensible (at the first) information. The universe seeks to know itself.

"The creation of something new is not accomplished by the intellect but by the play instinct acting from inner necessity. The creative mind plays with the objects it loves."
C. G. Jung, Psychological Types
Let’s revisit again that this study of the structure of unified, collective, and individual consciousness is a thought experiment. I am not a Sophos, a possessor of knowledge, but am a seeker on the way. Here are little objective facts. Nothing here can be proven. I am simply, as all philosophy has hitherto concerned itself, interested in being as such, and thus consciousness, which in order to exist must be conscious of a world and the universe it travels in.
When children become aware that some people are insane, that madness exists, often it becomes just another play thing. It is funny, and it is funny to pretend to be so. Like Clark’s proclamation that any technology which is sufficiently advanced will seem like magic to those who do not understand it, so too, for adults mostly, does awareness, concepts, feelings and systems of thought that are beyond the scope of understanding seem magical or spiritual or insane or dangerous. But this is serious play we are after here, always, and we invite the mystery and we are unremitting in the candor of our investigations of truth, we sojourners but never owners.
Spontaneous philosophy can be found in children and the insane, as Jaspers noted. We will be more rigorous within these pages, but keep that seriousness of play that is in all animals.
