Time here is analogous to the Feigenbaum Constant to the Mandelbrot Set, in that when seen sideways every nodule and bulb upon its finite parameter is itself a fractal, a period doubling bifurcation, splitting ever more in binary modes based on the universal tension of creation and unification, matter and antimatter, energy and dark energy, elements and compounds, light and its absence, living matter and nonliving matter, female and male, bilateral and radial and spherical biological symmetry, consciousness and bodies, awareness and objects of thought, good and evil, right and wrong, order and chaos, choices themselves, and other endless bifurcations and possibilities that require and are required by time. In these ways time moves forward, by yes or no answers to the questions life posits, as bits and qubits of information, the very fabric and textures of reality, whose information loops are seen on cellular and galactic and the scale of our consciousnesses normally, and like the acceleration of those period doubling bifurcations, the constant of 4.669 where when r = 3.75 chaos ensues in a semi-random pattern until the binary patterns restart, and so on as the rates increase, so time is speeding up in the universe and in we who age, so we have entropy, and that which gives times direction and its consequences, ever devolving into an homogenous simplified state of disorder. Consciousness, by its very connection to and essential nature made up of and as a generator of and meaning giver to patterns is anti-entropic, battling the slow dying of the universe as the epitome of life and as cheerleader and force for life.
16
Like a flashlight exposing objective structures in the dark, the light defined by the parameters and margins against the absence of light is as important as the boundaries and limits of the patterns of thought and the insoluble and impalpable and intangible dark and depths of consciousness itself, the light of our mental gestures and performances creating ever more patterns within which we experience and through which the creation learns more of itself. Light blending with light; consciousness slithering through the water’s bottom into the abyss, ever out of reach.
As all complex systems of thought are incomplete and inconsistent and will eventuate in their present state into antinomies and contradictions, as no intellectual system can prove the validity of its own structure from within, and therefore can both be confirmed and confuted, so neither can consciousness prove, validate or know its essential truth through reason alone.
It is only through living itself and the awareness thereof and in, and the awareness of that awareness, ad infinitum, as we swim through and utilize time, hands gaining more momentum when cupped and holding and giving and receiving than when fingers are spread in over-confidence or supplication, that we know the truth of our own consciousness as manifesta veritas, and thus the mysteries of ourselves and the whole cosmos we reflect.
17
One of the chief portents of creativity, that which asserts and reflects our life amongst ever more uncertainty and unease, for the world is not what it is, but what we are, as Anaïs Nin said, is not only its affirmation of the will to life, but also its vulnerability, what's left over when a piece of oneself is given to the public for inspection and judgement, one of the places where we achieve our humanity. It could be the personality one has formed through choosing oneself, which is choosing the world, as Sartre said, never careful enough in the pretending to realize that what we pretend will become us, as Vonnegut suggested, or participating in reflective gestures, spoken or performed, and in our mimicry we move emergent writhing with mental fur bristling and we wax and wane and crest over old ideas and stretch out and build from them and get stuck in their foundation; but that's one of the things that makes us human, that animal that is not a thing, that when we see we're in a box we step outside of it, now holding it, and thus the mental game continues with ever evolving rules. The box of the language game is inescapable, but there are other boxes within that must be sidled out of, including allowing space for vulnerability and epistemic hygiene and accepting reality, which means accepting the unknowns of existence without easy and lazy explanations, which includes us in those mysteries, which engenders our attempt to know them and gather the tools to work them out. Fortunately, there are people working on these things. Imagine being in a cage (or house, whatever - you are surrounded) where moving about inside you're presented constantly with puzzles, and if you don't solve them you become them, and when you do solve them you can see all of the potential interactions with the other hominins in the cage playing out beforehand, and that becomes your next puzzle, with the same rules, either stuck in a pattern or escaping and then into another and so on. Many simply surrender to their patterns.
Consciousness in existence is a necessary condition, and a sufficient condition in the human animal.
18
The reason we separate our intellect or awareness from feeling is because we are so easily emotionally swayed. We are not only reliant on expert testimony regarding the very frames and functions, including the plumbing and electrical systems of our abodes, and the engines and roads in which and on which we drive, and our phone applications and the music we listen to, as well as the atoms and their electrons and the protons and their quarks we believe in, and weather patterns and the stories of the monetary system, but most of the very structures within which we move day to day.
But we are also influenced by friends and family and politicians and social media and television and most everything else in our collective consciousness as the most social animals on the planet.
Our emotions, whether retrospective narratives or intensely immediate or ambient, is that which can be the most facilely influenced and coerced. Compassion and empathy are the inverse side of this coin. The nature of emotions is that they spread out into the world and conversely spread out within ourselves with the same methodology and construction. The nature to emotions is to feel. And that is the reason why the intellect detaches while always still attending to, by the very becoming aware of feeling, so that when we are being manipulated or influenced or swayed, we can have a conversation with what is doing the exploiting and look for truths and facts objectively, and predict the most beneficial outcome and act on that prediction.
At some point far back in the history of the human animal this transcendent awareness of emotions, perhaps due to a deception and tampering which would have forced one to act immorally or cruelly, occurred and spread like a virus with bestial heart, replicating as far as possible due to its selection for survival, but more its selection for goodness, virtue and the expansion of consciousness. Perhaps this was one of the first times we freely chose our evolution, no doubt surprising to all animals involved, and guided our becoming beyond instincts. And we have since never been the same, though we are never immune to the dangers of illusory emotional manipulation.
In such ways are metaphors evidence of applied epistemology, insofar as our mental gestures and intentions become in the act. We create as we think. We create as we become aware.
19
Somewhere there is a puddle after the rain on the roof of a garage, undulating with the wind, morphing and swaying like starlings, murmurations and quickenings, back and forth, rippling internally and extending outward, sessile in its flux, a confluence of both natural force and the grounding it has been laid upon, dark and pink and curved like a back out, and in the midst a man smokes after a good meal, and lends his breath to that flame out into the world, scent of wet pine, tobacco and petrichor, and in the end, after that hunger satisfied but always present and expected in the future, he maintains and recomposes, and leaves that magic for the next time.
Here ends open the second part of this thought experiment. Hopefully it wasn’t too discursive, these thoughts. In this succession of thoughts there are patterns as well. Ahead will be inspections of emergence, the history of thought, and the evolution of the universe.
I hope that you have taken seriously and playfully and lightly the invitation to participate in this journey and documentation.

sources.title: Parables of Silence, Part 3, Once the Pack, Now the Herd.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence, Chapter 3: Once the Pack, Now the Herd
“A mathematician, like a painter or poet, is a maker of patterns. If his patterns are more permanent than theirs, it is because they are made with ideas.”
G. H. Hardy
1
If time is the scaffolding and very structure of thought patterns, consciousness, by its very nature, segments and scissions time’s duration and flow into moments and ideas. If this is the natural and phenomenal creative constructions of consciousness, we also then use this differentiation, which inheres in the mind, with space itself, dividing existence into objects and things, and these we name and maybe more importantly, count. Existence is relational. These relationships and the limits of each individuation manifested, excluding all the rest, can be understood and defined by metaphors, and what recurs in them and between them are patterns, however impermanent and malleable. And this is the same for patterns of thought.
At the most fundamental level of comprehension is the story of reality itself, the story that best fits reality. And the narrative that is the most efficient and effective is mathematics, though the symbols, signs, characters, and metaphors we use for its diction are not nearly as important as those patterns and laws and truths in the rules of the game about which they describe.
Whatever words or symbols we were to use, the techniques, creatively constructed and descriptive of the relationships of existence, are necessary and integral aspects of consciousness’s project of understanding reality.
In short, mathematics and its use across disciplines, from physics and astronomy and chemistry and biology and economics, to building houses or bridges or cars or phones, might not be the ultimate truth, since the fundamental nature of reality is probably unknowable, but for our tiny minds it certainly describes and fits the mold of the patterns of existence the best, and allows us to creatively construct and move within and shape our reality.
2
“Shake dreams from your hair…”
Jim Morrison, Ghost Song
My mind sweats thinking about the responsibility and mandate to believe truths, rather than believing for the survival of the beliefs themselves, and concretizing abstractions by discussing them within appropriate circles or meditating on them within my thoughts, and by doing so making real certain cultural in-group duties toward right thinking about politics or religion or philosophy or morality, and thus disregarding truths for the sake of influence. On the obverse side of that coin, what is assumed to be more rational and less magical thinking is conspiratorial storylines, each attempting to create endings and closed systems and easy answers and beliefs and zero-point abstractions.
My mind sweats looking at how I myself have been manipulated into the storylines which make society possible, and that sweat is the water in which I swim and undulate, the puddle below the whole of an ocean, moving so human like, consciousness cresting and jetsams, but this metaphor, this stream of consciousness that’s a favorite for consciousness is simply that. Though consciousness over-satisfies its own conditions, it cannot prove itself from within using its own reason, and there is no water either.
There seems to be some contention about whether information, exchanged between all objects and things and matter and living organisms and intelligences, is something more than the mediums within which it travels and is transmitted and transmuted. But like entropy, that is all we can measure. There is no ghost in the machine. And of course bats are conscious. The medium is the message, as Marshall McLuhan said.
3
“El sueño de la razón produce monstruos.”
Francisco Goya
Rain drops and trickles down the murky and dim windows of our minds’ eyes. Outside the storm’s fluid dynamic shifts between inky, pitchless strain and gloom and lumination. On the inside, our mouths gape as wide as possible, lower jaws and lips dragged up the cold and damp pane with the viscosity of language. And we are the storm and its rain as well, though it too is as real as the need to communicate, to laugh at the ridiculousness of it all, to laugh in order to release the wells and dams of emotional pain, to scream when words won’t do and to tell our story.
Intelligence is nothing if not perceiving order from, and understanding that order and applying order to disorder and chaos. And we call both the perception and its orderings patterns. And the metaphors by which we call are patterns as well.
4
Depending on where you are on this planet, the sway of the pines in the moonlight or the turbo raised trucks whining past are all part of you. You are what you perceive.
If, at the end of your life, your story was replayed, it would document all that you perceived, including the mind aware of itself.
At the same time, the most real and immediate truth of existence is that we live in a world. A real world, outside of ourselves, inhabited by other soulful beings and inanimate objects. To be conscious is to be conscious of a world, as Husserl noted.
In these ways I am trying to show that, like the binary and dichotomous nature of reality and existence, the illusion is far ahead.
5
In discendo nova videmus, novas ideas, immo nova visa, sonos, et exempla. Realitas fundamentaliter ignota est, quamquam semper plus plusque eius videre possumus. Furor praeteriti fit libertas in natando, in saltando cum aeterno fluxu, numquam idem vir eundem pedem in eadem terra ponens, numquam ulla certitudo in ulla directione, ne domi quidem.
6
“My idea, as one can see, is that consciousness does not really belong to the existence of a man as an individual but rather to that in him which is community and herd; that, as follows from this, it has also evolved in refinement only with regard to usefulness for community and herd, and that consequently each of us, even with the best will to understand himself in as individual way as possible, ‘to know himself’, will nonetheless bring into his consciousness only what is not individual in him, his ‘average’.”
Nietzsche, The Gay Science, 354
It seems to me that we instinctively understand that there is order in the universe outside of our understanding. We are profoundly unaware, but posit the order that we perceive from the disorder of existence, the mental act of which is intelligence, and we assume or believe that this order pervades the universe itself.
Clarke said that any technology which is sufficiently beyond the mental scope of its witnesses will seem magical, and in such ways do our limited beliefs and imaginings proffer simple stories to explain that presupposed order beyond our collective and individual minds. We have been participating in magical thinking most likely at least for the last over 300,000 years and likely much longer.
In a way we’re right in thinking this way. For as soon as the quantum comes into existence in this story for which we have evidence, it becomes, as the rudimentary part of this story, a somewhere where nothing is completely random. Yet the more random things become the more complexity is maximized, and pattern recognition becomes harder, and thus predictability becomes more uncertain.
It may be the case that mathematics and patterns and beliefs and knowledge and predictability itself are simply our tiny minds throwing stories on a reality that is possibly unknowable. But, at least at this point, such living knowledge is at least efficacious. We not only move in this universe, we create in it too.
The great mystery of existence and all that we do not know impels us to learn and in the process create. As participant observers in our own becoming we are the creative animal, the believing animal, the seeking and learning animal, and the uncertain animal at our best. The most social animal on the planet. And the healing animal at our best.
7
Ubi dubium ibi libertas
The simpler, the more homogenous and similar in both parts and relationships, and therefore the more perfectly symmetrical, the more predictable and the more entropic. Fixed states, which are perhaps the most emblematic of the above mentioned, are therefore the most likely to decompose, dissolve and tend toward disorder.
But the more random, the more complex, and therefore the less predictable, ironically stabilizes the system and brings order to ever fluctuating states, at least by the growth of intelligence and awareness through learning, which themselves perceive order out of that which was previously disordered. Novel information engenders stability and is stabilized in turn by consciousness. The error in the system, its asymmetry and recursive uncertainty and instances of mutations and mistakes themselves allow for free will and consciousness and life themselves.
Around both of these polar opposites, namely, that which tries for fixed and closed systems, and that which is dynamic and kinematic and thus more stable through its becoming, is uncertainty. The more aware the mind becomes of reality, the more there is an awareness of uncertainty, which is the engine, that is, curiosity, for learning itself. In fact we might say that coupled with the first moments of awareness is the awareness of the unknown and all the emotive rationalizations that that supposes - the monster under the bed to the fear of what comes after death, as Ethan Campbell noted.
It is by the expansion of awareness, fed by information and learning, a process which can never end, never reach final answers, an awareness that can grow and expand and widen like the expansion of the universe itself, ever faster, that we become more and more fully (but not entirely) aware that we will never know it all; in essence, the more we know the more we know that we don’t know. Or, more simply, our ignorance not only unfolds infinitely before our expanding awareness, but grows in proportion to the increase and widening and clarity of our intellect.
In these ways we are simultaneously a speck of dust and all that we can imagine. We are at once the smallest and the largest things we can mentally capture and picture. And we feel with every thought.
8
The medium is the message. Our thoughts are not dissociated nor separate from our bodies and this world. Every thought, idea and concept and mental attitude and intention and gesture and performance has an effect on the world, and they have an effect on our bodies as well. That can be our health or even simply the lines on our faces. But most importantly, this internal world, which is inseparable from feeling, even if we are aware of that feeling, because we are aware of it, informs our behavior, which creates ripples across, mainly, our collective social species, as well as well beyond what we can comprehend.
9
Occam’s Razor, the simpler answer or technique or solution, like the mandate to use simpler words when writing, is consciousness seeing order out of disorder. It is that which stabilizes the system. Order is not necessarily complex. Most of the time it is simple. But in order not to succumb to the simple disorder of entropy, consciousness takes a necessarily complex situation or pattern or state of reality and makes sense of it by forming ordered patterns out of, and perceiving ordered patterns from that randomness. And in at least some cases , simplification of the complex, like the art of solutions, brings temporary stability within the uncertainty and fluctuation, a stability which must be actualized over and over and endlessly by taking in and ordering complex and novel information continuously.
The trend toward the simplified disorder of entropy is countered by the meaningful ordering of complex and new information, taken in and perceived and understood as simply and clearly as possible. We need both the asymmetry and randomness and complexity and the less predictable to work the engines of our minds, to create new insightful and comprehensive knowledge and understanding, to create new patterns, to create itself. The act of perceiving order from a previously disordered state simplifies it so that it is intelligible.
10
There would be no thoughts without a prior time and a succeeding time. Without segmenting time and space into discreet elements we would experience sensory overload and mush. We would be in a state of pure absorption and reactions. There would be no plumbing, no instinct nor need for order, no simultaneous creating.
Tentative stability achieved and received through ordering reality is itself awareness, emerging from myriad fundamental properties, which perceives itself by capturing moments through temporal slicing. Awareness thus witnesses itself in relation to itself through scissioning duration, like it sees itself in relation to the world that it is conscious of, pockets of which allow room for thought, which themselves take time and utilize time as its scaffolding and structure, defined by the limits that are the patterns of thought, at least some of which are the limits that are the segments of time, that is, before and after.
11
There is a map. Focusing and centering in and zooming in on its coastline results in interminable repeating self-similar patterns. The fjords are reflected in the inlets. All the way in and all the way out the limits and boundaries self-replicate.  The continent is the content and context of our thoughts. Therein texture unfolds, and sounds and colors. All is metaphor. All are stories. Senses are used to describe the other senses. The narratives are circular. Shapes are everywhere. The shapes of the patterns of these thoughts are the coastline. The stream, the water, the ocean which is the stream in which consciousness builds itself and plays and splashes is time. And the map is not the territory. The territory is endless and unknown, full of uncertainty and ignorance, ultimately unknowable, where we fall off the edge over and again in our expansion into the abyss, which grows exponentially with our incursions, farther and faster away. Still, we are the universe discovering itself even as we unendingly and methodically and with mathematic precision step out of mental boxes by becoming aware of them, perceiving order, recognizing patterns by differentiating time and space, rocking the boat and getting our hair wet, sometimes so quietly, discovering ourselves in the process.
As we say, “I am no longer a child. I need to know what you know.”
12
And now here we have come upon the contradictions that we have discussed above and knew would eventually happen and were inevitable. Not simply paradoxes. Previously in Part 1 we had refuted James’ idea that thoughts are islands in the stream of consciousness. Perhaps like water, these are the metaphors best able to fit reality, however their inability to touch or encapsulate existence completely.
The Innsbruck Goggle Experiment has been going on since the late 19th century. Essentially subjects are given goggles that, while worn, turn the world upside down. Within a matter of days the brain adapts, and some are even able to ride bicycles, even while perceiving the sky at their feet and the ground above their heads. Afterward, it takes a few days for the brain to adjust back to normal. But adjust it does. We are highly adaptable. There are humans in the very hottest and coldest places on the planet, far below the ocean’s surface and 250 miles up in space. We are able to adapt and improvise our pattern recognitions for survival. And we thrive.
If all of reality is the consciousness of God, which is our metaphor for the great mystery and enunciation of a universal order we cannot perceive or understand but that we instinctively feel and believe, and creation, or the Source, or the Abyss, that consciousness is manifested materially, measurably, physically, and is as such reflected in and as our own minds. Whether we have souls or not is immeasurable, and can neither be proven nor disproved. It is a metaphysical question. But we know because we experience in ourselves as well of others that we are soulful beings. We feel soulfully.
As you may have noticed, the circumference of this journey of documentation and dialogue between us is widening, while the same topics keep coming to the fore, like the abyss mentioned above. Much like a spiral, the circle expands outward while at the same time turning inward on each topic as they arise. In this way you see my own consciousness working. And although the content of these words, these incantations, are paramount, the limits and patterns and structure of the text is important as well.
As was mentioned in Part 1, entropy in the physical definition is a complexity and randomness of energy that dissipates toward disorder. I mentioned this when speaking of pattern recognition with regard to IQ tests. However, since time’s direction will ultimately result in the universe’s heat death, whether that be luke warm or freezing, the end game is a universal state of homogeneity. This is not the only key to understanding the reversal of the definition where it concerns human consciousness. The key is that the trend toward disorder happens within closed systems. And closed systems in the human mind are fixed systems, symmetrical systems, and predictable systems.
Mental entropy is in some ways an inversion of the process of the entropy of physics. Simple and static systems lead to disorder and illness. Not only does consciousness utilize time as its structure, like the fact that thoughts take time, like consciousness dividing time into segments, but the mind also utilizes time by ordering and simplifying the highly complex and random and unpredictable nature of its inherent physical entropy to affirm life, the quintessential paragon of which is consciousness.
Consciousness uses time, in which it is trapped irreversibly every way it turns, and creatively utilizes it every way it can to fight for life and understanding and creation and affirmation of sentient being itself. It is a war that so far we have lost every combatant’s life to. Still, we must persist.
And for all of this we do not operate with or on certainty, but probability. The insights of Gödel and Hume and Kant have been invaluable for stepping out of those boxes.
13
“Life is one member of a class of phenomena which are open or continuous reaction systems able to decrease their internal entropy at the expense of free energy taken in from the environment and subsequently rejected in degraded form.”
JD Bernal, The Physical Basis of Life
Herein we have been sojourning into questions about questions, that is, what would these metaphysical concepts like God look like, what was before the Big Bang, what is time and free will and consciousness? And more. It’s in the very act of hypothetical questioning itself that gives us this space to explore and try to give structure and thus context to these ideas. The questions, “What would it look like if these things did exist? What would they look like as facts and natural phenomena?” allows our creative animal minds to find patterns and purport theoretical definitions, both of which supply meaning.
The very act of positing the questions substantiates the possibility for open ended answers, however incomplete.
Like every other thought system, this new creative world will eventuate into contradictions and antinomies. It will be incomplete and inconsistent and uncertain. And so we step out of these parameters, as well as the new parameters outside of those old, over and over, by the realization that we are in another contained system.
It is because our consciousnesses are open before the universe that we have the intellect that we do, however minuscule. The potential is astounding, just based on what little we know.
The second law of thermodynamics suggests that in closed systems that are large enough, that don’t share any energy with anything outside of itself, the energy within will either remain constant or diffuse into disorder. But for open systems large enough, as Krauss mentioned, a system taking and sharing and giving with its environment, exchanging energy between the borders of its ever changing limits, as in the subsistence and waste of living organisms, life can grow and expand and flourish.
This is how consciousness uses time. This is how life itself works, and this is how consciousness as the epitome of life makes use of the world in which it finds itself in order to find and perceive order and learn and become more aware.
Entropy naturally trends toward a low state of energy - my coffee gets cold, my tobacco dries - and so it is with fixed mental states, closed and erroneous mental systems, easy answers to the prominent questions life asks of us, and the assumption of complete beliefs. Eventually, in such states, there is little energy left over for use, and the system falls into disorder.
All of this means the fight for the future of life is not futile. It is a worthy goal, and it is a rational and instinctual imperative.
Here ends open Part 3 of A Parable of Silence. Next we will, as we have here, be getting a bit more technical. In our journey together we will regather in the following pages and begin to look at the mechanisms of life and thus consciousness more closely, from which it emerges and follows naturally.

sources.title: Parables of Silence, Part 4, Round the Cage.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence, Chapter 4: Round the Cage
“God is a sphere whose center is everywhere and whose circumference is nowhere.”
St. Bonaventure
“The eye by which I see God is the same as the eye by which God sees me. My eye and God’s eye are one and the same.”
Meister Eckhart
1
This is not an empirical study. But there is efficacy in the hypothetical and asking the utility of concepts rather than their unprovable truths. If you feel skepticism, lean into that. This system remains open for both of us.
This is an attempt to study the architecture of consciousness and thoughts and more. This widening of the circumferences are not changes necessarily in the focal contents, but our perspectives. There are indispensable constraints in these feedback loops, fed by eventual contradictions and inconsistencies, which then lead to a transcendent step outside of that state of thought, whose uncertainty then motivates new creations and perceptions or order from disorder, and the process repeats unendingly, hopefully. We call this the reflex-arc of intention.
The limits and boundaries of these thoughts are fractal and replicable on every scale, and these are the sounds and colors and shapes and textures of our thought patterns. For instance, if you have a string in a vacuum and strum it, the sound will be fast lost. The same is true with other sounds, like lips vibrating in a total vacuum. But put them on or in a shape, as on a violin, or in a trombone, and in a room with windows either opened or closed, and the string or horn gives off a certain frequency and tone and feeling. And of course these change based off the tension we put on that string or how we purse our lips, and the textures, the smooth brass of the horn or the wood of the violin and string and lips themselves. And as we are able to visualize that violin and trombone, so there are colors of thoughts, whether that violin is brown and that metal gold or otherwise in your mind. And these patterns are strewn throughout reality, because they are that which recur in space and time through repetition, which means they’re predictable, and we therefore, through an impetus much like the universe’s need to differentiate and know itself, apply and give meaning to them automatically. In such ways does consciousness apply and perceive order from disorder. In such ways we perceive patterns throughout reality, which are therefore mathematical. And although it might be that these perceptions are just a story we blanket over reality, and not reality in and of itself, these perceptions are useful, not only for understanding and comprehension, but for molding and creating and building and fitting that mold of and over and within reality. And the limits and boundaries make repetition possible.
We can only do these things because life is both an exchange of energy and information with its environment, and is therefore an open system, but as an open system increases order at the expense of that environment, as Krauss wrote.
Still, we crave, as the believing animal, since we are separated from most of our knowledge and must trust experts, which may have started with the use of metaphors for existence, or language itself, a step in being aware of being aware, closed systems, which are mentally entropic and lead to disorder. The only cure for this, the only natural remedy to keep the system open, and therefore life affirming and negentropic is to learn. That is the only thing that never fails. It is the only thing we never regret.
But here’s the catch. The uncertainty of the mind and world upon awaking from sleep is tentatively stabilized through innate meaning and curiosity and the learning that is exigent. The other animals, many of whom are intelligent, move in the world naturally. As far as we know, they do not have to think about this process.
But upon the awareness of awareness and so on, through the awareness of our own ultimate freedom of our potential to participate in our becoming, we become responsible for those same meanings, curiosity and ensuing need to learn. As our awareness expands so does our freedom, which is based in and on our responsibility to create ourselves. The only way to stay sane under the weight of this frightful responsibility is to approach existence itself with serious play, with honesty and humility, and with commitment and infinite interest and curiosity.
But mistakes will be made. And the regret that follows is the other example of our free will. For we could have chosen differently. And this is the basis for all ethical systems. The inevitable mistakes are required by a system whose information is born from an error and is required in order to learn. It is required by a system in order to remain open. The error is required for life itself.
2
What we call spirit is the feeling of our consciousness. Emotion, which is feeling, and even feeling feeling, by definition feels things, not only having the feeling but feeling itself. And this is not the ambience and atmosphere of feeling with every thought. In this instance it is consciousness itself being felt. How others feel that through empathy is not defined by the individual whose consciousness is being felt. We cannot control what others feel from or about us. Within our most intimate feeling of ourselves is our inability to control how others may perceive that feeling. And that spiritual feeling coexists with consciousness, and not only comes from the awareness of existing, but the mystery of ourselves, to ourselves, as the universe is a mystery unto itself, as every scale of self-replicating patterns in a fractal is a reflection of those scales above and below, as our consciousnesses are a reflection of the universe as a whole.
One of the most excruciating pains is the always changing nature of that feeling as we grow and with age. We trade naturally innocence for knowledge and freedom and wisdom.
3
When we think herein of the Source and the creation of time and space, and the infinite individuations existing in and having gravitational pulls on that time and space, we are thinking of the Big Bang and what came before. But the direction of time and the entropic tendency toward low energy and disorder is moving toward the collapse back into that single unified and utterly unique singular source defined by the absence of everything else, and therefore defined by nothing, and thus erasing reality in the process.
The original tension apparent in all of creation between this endgame of time, and consciousness itself, is a friction, the existence of which requires movement, which itself necessitates time and space. The cosmos and its need to know itself compels creation.
We talked earlier about free will, in one sense being verified by regret, the knowledge that we could have chosen differently. Consciousness narrates the past, looking backward and projecting the belief that there could have been other choices made onto fixed and already determined timelines. Consciousness narrates the past, and believes in the truth of subjective freedom, and applies those lessons to inform the choosing we do constantly while living forward. This is an affirmation and a knowing of the subjective reality of free will.
In the duality of binary reality, mental entropy is manifested either as static and fixed and simple states, or too much noise, too many patterns colliding, too much disorder in the unpredictable onslaught of undifferentiated data. Both are failed attempts and projects to become time itself, and not to simply use it for the scaffolding of consciousness, but to capture what we utilize, to exist on the one hand, and to flow forever without interruption, or at least to experience it fully, on the other. And these can be instinctual and impulsive intentions toward and for oblivion.
And time is entropic, destroying matter, perhaps slowly relative to our perspective, so slowly that we gloss over it. But if consciousness is negentropic, anti-entropic, and an affirmation of life fighting for survival, then in what direction is it battling for and toward?
If consciousness is fractal and made of patterns, which uses time for its structure, as we mentioned above, it’s possible that time is the sideways view of those fractals, its flow created so often and ever again by choices between either most often opposites and dual and dichotomous and the binary nature of existence, or as often happens, choices between trifurcations and quadrifications or more. But choices nonetheless, or even collapses by observations generating seemingly seamless duration.
What this looks like on the macro scale of fractals of consciousness, insofar as direction or dimensions are concerned, is unknown. And all of this is metaphor and analogous and analogue.
4
The skin of cuttlefish and octopuses and squid are inversely proportional to our minds, insofar as their problem solving for survival, and the mimicry of their learning, as well as their serious play, their malleability and adaptabilities, and their immediate creations of color, textures, sounds and shapes and patterns through reflex intentions, are inside out reversals of our inner minds, are all expressions of the same source of consciousness. Their boundaries and the limits of their thought patterns are their own bodies, skin, mantel.
In this way we are able to witness that intelligence and consciousness so defined are existent throughout and around us in different individuations and manifestations of life in the universe. Elsewhere outside of our species, other species express patterns and their limits and content and intention much the same way, ordering disorder, in various and dissimilar and sometimes inverse or stranger forms.
5
Often older persons will look at the young and watch how their minds are moving. Especially when younger people approach new problems to solve. This is normally a very joyful experience.
It is just like this that awareness expands, that learning changes perception, sight, and mental techniques. The simpler structures of former states of consciousness seem immature, even if necessary, and base and basic perceptions are often tied to the sickness of the ego and superficial desires. As we ever more expand our awareness, it is not that we lose our ego or ascend to a higher plane or become old wizards or magical. No, we simply have more freedom and details and information with every transcendence of the former cage. We then are rewarded with a horizon far more vast and an ocean far deeper, all of which only speak one sentence: You are profoundly unaware, staggeringly ignorant, and there is infinitely more to learn.
6
When I was nine-years-old and in 5th grade, my best friend and I made up our own language. Most of it had the quality of making us belly laugh. We still use it to this day.
Language as metaphor is ever changing and creating new words. Each generation adds to it, like a kind of pigeon language within language that is, after a time, assimilated. Whether because we are accelerating exponentially at this point in our evolution, most of which most of us are unaware of, or that language was always never enough, language as metaphor is circular, like our conversations and thoughts, in some ways because of its confines. It is constrained by its limits. Even schizophrenics’ word salad is circular, often tight and taut. If we had language that not only kept up and followed time, generating new words and sentences and syntax upon every moment of inspiration…well, we can barely imagine what access to the source and existence and what elastic knowledge would be required for such a feat.
For instance, there are thousands and more instances where, not simply because language is metaphorical and doesn’t quite touch existence, but more because what we use tends to not only explain other words, but that ideas themselves require words borrowed from other areas within language.
For example, we use sensory words describing touch, smell, sight, taste, sound and feelings, to describe other sensory ideas and emotions and senses. The roaring fire, the sharp mind, light and heavy emotions, dim and slow and quick and clear and cool thinking, flashes of insight, earthy taste and smells, a scratchy or velvety voice or sound, a rough blanket, a sweet or vivacious personality, a chilly or sour or bitter or dazzling or gloomy temperament. And we use words this way all over the map of language, even for the most technical professions, as in charm and strange quarks, and the building blocks and code of and book of life of DNA, the factories of cells and cells as logic gates, and heat engines and waves of light, switches and oscillators, and equations as keys and the instruments and games of logic.
The metaphors that make up language are self referential. Its system is open and always evolving. But these systems are closed only insofar as they are inadequate to fully explain and define the meaning we experience in ourselves and in the world and universe. One area of expertise that reminds us of this is poetry, a technique of many styles that most attempt at some point, and the best show us that their exodus is within, and who reach for the ineffable, as Sharon Olds once wrote.
7
Evolution is activated, powered, charged, and propelled by failure and learning, and the consequent modicum of modifications and then presentation of change over time.
We are made of matter that is slowly dying from the start, but matter that also embodies consciousness, which struggles against the decay of time. The best weapon we have come up with so far has been the ability to give birth, to make babies, to love in the instant in hopefully most cases, and to give birth to new life, creating an ongoing cycle, even as our consciousness rebirths ceaselessly.
This instability equates to kinetic friction, which is the consequence of the fundamental tension of this universe, namely, the skirmish for life versus the decomposition adjoined and constitutive of time. This tension, this abrasion, this angst and discord, and thus this friction, is reflected in everything that exists. Friction necessitates motion, the battle is motile, and thus space and time, which is being generated through every choice and also through every possibility that those choices never actuate and leave behind. Time is the rate, felt subjectively, of this process. This is another way consciousness uses time to engage and combat time. We must assume time would exist without consciousness, as we assume many other animals experience time without the above mentioned scaffolding, piece by piece, bar by bar, that we use and make out of time.
The endgame of the error that gives birth to every thing and mind is such that it is important that an acceptance that the error is within us, and not necessarily out in the world, is paramount and essential to learning and enlarging and amplifying and dilating awareness. We fight this ever present uncertainty and instability through learning, even as it is a necessary survival function. Herein meaning is the shortcut insofar as, if one is aware of patterns, one can survive in them. Even flourish. Patterns are not only tools but also constraints that allow for context and meaning. The whole system is a form of what we might call information management, driven by the need to bring and perceive order from disorder, as life increases order at the expense of its environment, an open system, as emotions are the sensations of consciousness, as we feel our own minds.
8
The exploitation of its environment by life is another way that animals and our species harm one another. For we do not live in an environment, as Dewey said, we are part of one. As an example, the most prolific economic system on our planet exploits individuals and the environment wherever it can at the expense of those persons and systems. On the smaller scale, we fulfill what we are naturally compelled to do, that is, work, create, and be productive in shaping our reality. In the mesoscopic to macroscopic arena, exploitation that syphons the benefits of the system by that system’s very rules toward a few, contends with the drive for a system that will equally benefit all, with the former winning in every economic system that has hitherto existed. Like all systems, it is open, and we do not seek perfection but expansion. For all of this, many more people now more than ever are more prosperous or at least have enough.
However, it is presumed that civilization began with empathy and compassion and care for others. This natural tendency toward sociability and affection is linked directly to learning and serious play. The connections we feel with others are intense instances of interpersonal relationships, and small social circles are of a type that we call love. These instincts are such that there are now more than 8 billion human animals on this planet.
Instinct as reactive unconscious behavior, with the growth and widening and clarity of awareness, may used as an inspection device, but because we are conscious of them, by definition we introduce errors. Most other animals seem to rely and embody instinctive purpose and interactions fluidly and correctly and truthfully in every sense of their being. But when we become aware of our instincts, and inspect them, we become as it were shamans born in the wrong time. Communing with spirits. Spirits some of whom tell us to assume not only that they are correct interpretations of reality, filtered through consciousness, and sometimes reality itself, but also that they can become and predict the future. These narratives the mind tells itself are reinforced by being sometimes correct. But we gloss over and forget that this misapplication and misconception and misstep not only leads to mistakes, but at the extreme can also lead to unjustified conflict and delusion. This is a miscarriage of the duty to see far enough inside of ourselves, to witness the insolvency, and this negligence can bankrupt us. And we see and perceive more and more clearly by taking in ever more information, both internally and externally. And we call this learning.
9
There would be no fractals, and thus no consciousness as we know it, without shapes and patterns, which are recurring differentiating universals, and in this way what they contain and what they exclude make their replication possible.
All this acute life it seems I have been a refugee fleeing myself in search of making a camp in myself around this playfully devouring firelight - fleeing myself toward myself, round and round the cage.

sources.title: Parables of Silence, Part 5, The Squall on the Palm.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence: Chapter 5
The Squall on the Palm



T.S. Eliot, Four Quartets, 1: Burnt Norton

I

Time present and time past
Are both perhaps present in time future,
And time future contained in time past.
If all time is eternally present
All time is unredeemable.
What might have been is an abstraction
Remaining a perpetual possibility
Only in a world of speculation.
What might have been and what has been
Point to one end, which is always present.
Footfalls echo in the memory
Down the passage which we did not take
Towards the door we never opened
Into the rose-garden. My words echo
Thus, in your mind.
But to what purpose
Disturbing the dust on a bowl of rose-leaves
I do not know.
Other echoes
Inhabit the garden. Shall we follow?
Quick, said the bird, find them, find them,
Round the corner. Through the first gate,
Into our first world, shall we follow
The deception of the thrush? Into our first 
world.
There they were, dignified, invisible,
Moving without pressure, over the dead leaves,
In the autumn heat, through the vibrant air,
And the bird called, in response to
The unheard music hidden in the shrubbery,
And the unseen eyebeam crossed, for the 
roses
Had the look of flowers that are looked at.
There they were as our guests, accepted and 
accepting.
So we moved, and they, in a formal pattern,
Along the empty alley, into the box circle,
To look down into the drained pool.
Dry the pool, dry concrete, brown edged,
And the pool was filled with water out of 
sunlight,
And the lotos rose, quietly, quietly,
The surface glittered out of heart of light,
And they were behind us, reflected in the pool.
Then a cloud passed, and the pool was empty.
Go, said the bird, for the leaves were full of 
children,
Hidden excitedly, containing laughter.
Go, go, go, said the bird: human kind
Cannot bear very much reality.
Time past and time future
What might have been and what has been
Point to one end, which is always present.


II

Garlic and sapphires in the mud
Clot the bedded axle-tree.
The trilling wire in the blood
Sings below inveterate scars
Appeasing long forgotten wars.
The dance along the artery
The circulation of the lymph
Are figured in the drift of stars
Ascend to summer in the tree
We move above the moving tree
In light upon the figured leaf
And hear upon the sodden floor
Below, the boarhound and the boar
Pursue their pattern as before
But reconciled among the stars.

At the still point of the turning world. Neither 
flesh nor fleshless;
Neither from nor towards; at the still point, 
there the dance is,
But neither arrest nor movement. And do not 
call it fixity,
Where past and future are gathered. Neither 
movement from nor towards,
Neither ascent nor decline. Except for the point, 
the still point,
There would be no dance, and there is only the 
dance.
I can only say, there we have been: but I cannot 
say where.
And I cannot say, how long, for that is to place 
it in time.
The inner freedom from the practical desire,
The release from action and suffering, release 
from the inner
And the outer compulsion, yet surrounded
By a grace of sense, a white light still and 
moving,
Erhebung without motion, concentration
Without elimination, both a new world
And the old made explicit, understood
In the completion of its partial ecstasy,
The resolution of its partial horror.
Yet the enchainment of past and future
Woven in the weakness of the changing body,
Protects mankind from heaven and damnation
Which flesh cannot endure.
Time past and time future
Allow but a little consciousness.
To be conscious is not to be in time
But only in time can the moment in the rose-
garden,
The moment in the arbour where the rain beat,
The moment in the draughty church at 
smokefall
Be remembered; involved with past and future.
Only through time time is conquered.

III

Here is a place of disaffection
Time before and time after
In a dim light: neither daylight
Investing form with lucid stillness
Turning shadow into transient beauty
With slow rotation suggesting permanence
Nor darkness to purify the soul
Emptying the sensual with deprivation
Cleansing affection from the temporal.
Neither plenitude nor vacancy. Only a flicker
Over the strained time-ridden faces
Distracted from distraction by distraction
Filled with fancies and empty of meaning
Tumid apathy with no concentration
Men and bits of paper, whirled by the cold wind
That blows before and after time,
Wind in and out of unwholesome lungs
Time before and time after.
Eructation of unhealthy souls
Into the faded air, the torpid
Driven on the wind that sweeps the gloomy hills 
of London,
Hampstead and Clerkenwell, Campden and 
Putney,
Highgate, Primrose and Ludgate. Not here
Not here the darkness, in this twittering world.

Descend lower, descend only
Into the world of perpetual solitude,
World not world, but that which is not world,
Internal darkness, deprivation
And destitution of all property,
Desiccation of the world of sense,
Evacuation of the world of fancy,
Inoperancy of the world of spirit;
This is the one way, and the other
Is the same, not in movement
But abstention from movement; while the world 
moves
In appetency, on its metalled ways
Of time past and time future.


IV

Time and the bell have buried the day,
The black cloud carries the sun away.
Will the sunflower turn to us, will the clematis
Stray down, bend to us; tendril and spray
Clutch and cling?

    Chill
Fingers of yew be curled
Down on us? After the kingfisher's wing
Has answered light to light, and is silent, the 
light is still
At the still point of the turning world.


V

Words move, music moves
Only in time; but that which is only living
Can only die. Words, after speech, reach
Into the silence. Only by the form, the pattern,
Can words or music reach
The stillness, as a Chinese jar still
Moves perpetually in its stillness.
Not the stillness of the violin, while the note 
lasts,
Not that only, but the co-existence,
Or say that the end precedes the beginning,
And the end and the beginning were always 
there
Before the beginning and after the end.
And all is always now. Words strain,
Crack and sometimes break, under the burden,
Under the tension, slip, slide, perish,
Decay with imprecision, will not stay in place,
Will not stay still. Shrieking voices
Scolding, mocking, or merely chattering,
Always assail them. The Word in the desert
Is most attacked by voices of temptation,
The crying shadow in the funeral dance,
The loud lament of the disconsolate chimera.

    The detail of the pattern is movement,
As in the figure of the ten stairs.
Desire itself is movement
Not in itself desirable;
Love is itself unmoving,
Only the cause and end of movement,
Timeless, and undesiring
Except in the aspect of time
Caught in the form of limitation
Between un-being and being.
Sudden in a shaft of sunlight
Even while the dust moves
There rises the hidden laughter
Of children in the foliage
Quick now, here, now, always—
Ridiculous the waste sad time
Stretching before and after.



1

Ignari sumus, et ignorantes manebimus

The danger is not that we are in a box, or cage, of our own making. We’re always within the limits prescribed by our intentions. The danger is not recognizing that we are there interned. Belief in immutable truth is decay, and with its rigidity there is eventual breakdown, entropy, energy dissipating in the closed system. That is what contributes to our own linear decomposition.

Contradictions and inconsistencies impel us to create a more informed and complex and honest system of thought to account for a reality our consciousness edits into patterns, by both ordering disorder and perceiving those patterns, turning noise into signals and tempos and lyrics that can be replicated as naturally as the rest of the laws of the universe. Awareness is a filter that is complex and insightful and full of error, which is required to learn and keep the system open. In every ascension of meaning, every widening awareness of information, we are simply becoming ever more honest with ourselves and the rest of creation.

2

The man has forgotten to eat. He doesn't feel the pangs of hunger any longer. He smokes outside, like a caryatid holding up the house, plumes rising like dragons weighted down by her draped body, toes showing her age. It is cold outside. Across the wall the shades are open, and he's afraid they'll think he's watching them, crazy voyeur making murderous plans. So he looks toward the planets and stars, willing himself beyond himself and into far minds unknowable, breathing in as much of the universe as his imagining can occur, and out onto the senses of his surroundings like the cloth of truth. It is heavy outside. Inside he is like a feather, striped black and brown like a hawk's, layered in memories and relievos of younger, happier and whole times against scents of environmental recollection, down to the vibrating bone. There, in the midst of tap-dancing and tumbling leaves scraping around his sandals and the chilly night's lumens, he plans his escape, his exodus within, backing out of a moment of peace into a choir of his own making, and shuts the door.

3

Serious play does not take away from either the gravity, nor the light of a situation. In fact, we’re practicing it all the time. We playfully create stories about ourselves and the world, often unaware, which may be why tricksters are so prominent in creation stories across the globe. We pretend who we want to be, picking ourselves out of the cultural narratives available to us, until even we believe it. In the pretending and choosing we are free as children. Fortunately for most, there are rarely consequences. Others believe what our outward stories relay, what clothes we wear, how we hold ourselves, just as we do their’s. This oils society. And at the grandest scales, unto our own minds, we normally believe in some mythology, which are public dreams, even as our dreams are private myths, as Campbell noted. Our economic and religious and cultural narratives leave us just enough time to work, play, choose, and invent our personalities.

But if we are honest enough to shed those fables about ourselves and the worlds of our species, if we are rigorously honest enough to create ever more open systems, we can then laugh at ourselves for our blunders, and take the greatest joy in our creations and continuous and infinite need to learn, and in the learning itself.

And of course, as any parent or caretaker of another animal can tell you, personalities are encoded from the start. But we cannot seem to let ourselves be, as Staley sung. We are ever revising ourselves. Still, it’s our responsibility to embrace and sponsor our freedom and create meaning, rather than personalities, which are already present but always transformable and lean towards metamorphosis, but never replacement. This need to buy our personalities is one of the myriad ways our mythologies are fed.

4

By now you will have noticed that we covered similar topics in Part 2, and therein we built an epistemology out of Part 1, which was our mythology of sorts, and out of that epistemology we’ve constructed our metaphysics. But the modeling of this process is where we find our value, rather than an attempt at irrefutable truths which are inherently incomplete and contradictory, as is the case with belief systems and philosophy and logic and mathematics and ethics and politics and economics and the rest. 

Here we are tapping into the very structures and rules of consciousness, which in our minds are designed to read patterns, with logic and rationality and belief built in. We can do this because of the repetition of universals, or because recurring forms in space and time reliably recur, and are thus predictable, which we instinctively apply meaning to as coexistent with and the content of information. But, these tools are flawed from the start. And that flaw allows for change and growth. The goal is indistinguishable from our current projects, but underneath it is movement and a project for life and consciousness, with an unclear and unknowable end because it may be infinite, hopefully.

Complexity reaches a level of mathematical chaos, and renormalization, or, awareness of the box by becoming aware of its incompleteness and contradictions, and thus transcending the box into a more expansive box, for the time being, and brings stability through higher ordering of more complex information. This process permeates our very essence. In this view the fact that we will always be profoundly ignorant, and more so the more we know, is not a personal failure, but the engine for that expansion and the excitement within the ensuing infinite curiosity, which grows exponentially in accordance with our awareness of just how stupendously unaware we truly are.

All the while we have been discussing the importance of the limits and boundaries of patterns, given form by intentions. But the snapshots, the scissions out of time, are also the limits of our patterns of thoughts. The act is less like scissors and more like a sculptor, for within these apprehensions out of time are time sculpted into the forms of patterns of thoughts, which are the textures and colors and shapes and sounds of consciousness. The infinite potential of our minds made intelligible through those limits exists as an extension of protuberances of time, utilizing time to counteract time’s decomposition and affirm awareness and life. 

Time is often considered by our mental systems as an external medium, but in the assumption of its fleeting and ever changing nature can be witnessed its instability except when we subjectively apply intention to sculpt form from its infinite potential.

5 

There are a great many who would adopt and procure scientific ideas, especially these days from physics, to explain spiritual techniques to acquire the most beneficial things, people, and experiences into their own lives. For instance, attraction by thinking seems to be popular at the moment. But of course there are starving children around the world who must be thinking endlessly about food and who get little of it. All systems of thought are open, fortunately, and thus flawed.

Of course, we do seem to attract what we pay attention to. This is not because our thoughts are magnets, but because we notice and follow mentally things we are either attracted to, or have just learned of, or want to become.

And it is just like this inversion of awareness of our own perception that we grab and mold time. We fit the mold we create.

Another trend, this one thousands of years old, has been a passion for becoming disinterested in the world and experiences. Of course there are moments where this is very effective at keeping ourselves sane. But in totality it betrays living and experiencing itself, while assuming some higher order state of being which is itself caged by its very own structure. As ultimate certainty breeds embarrassment and sometimes catastrophe, so does puffing and lauding up our special qualities, especially those techniques we’ve learned, lead to disappointments and fumbled foundering.

6

Time is often seen as something to be aware of, an addition, a makeweight, a fact that impresses itself upon us, a road whereon we make memories along the way, a place we furrow and burrow into, and something to be exploited and taken for granted, or that of which we have too much or not enough of.

But time is coterminous to consciousness, and there is no consciousness without time, and our intentions sculpt ephemeral and protean time to create the forms and limits and boundaries of thoughts, and their reliably recurring patterns.

The point at which consciousness instinctively grasps time is as a mantissa, or more specifically a significant, a floating decimal, this focal point with a gargantuan range of possibilities in scope, from however many times we wish to split a second, to the grandest scale we can imagine. And it is one of the emergent properties of consciousness, that is, imaginings, from which these scales emerge themselves.

The focal point of awareness generates the intention, and the intention directs the awareness, and this is an underlying level of recursive attachment to time in the reflex-arc of intention.

The meridians within these moments, meridians here being both senses, that connect and synthesize disparate and common concepts and ideas and intuitions, are thereby creating the pathways within these moments and the continent of thoughts.

This is not an interpolation of time by consciousness, nor vice versa, but both are dependent on the other - consciousness, for its very existence, and time, for the awareness of itself, as well as the eventual result of its dynamic process, that is - life is the result of time’s becoming.

7

Nonlinear dynamic decision matrices, river deltas, trees, lightning, mountains, coastlines, blood vessels, neural networks…

Patterns in fractals are left as consciousness separates itself from the intensity and terrifying pain and anxiety of the body, by the same means as the intellect separates itself from emotions, that is, by being aware of the feeling. In the loss of oneness with the body through loss of most of its connection to it, the body becomes something that sends messages, that we feel, and, in some cases, a vessel that we tolerate and feed and clean. 

We have come to this point because we are the prey species. The fear and the terror of the errors that would cause us to lose our lives, or our loved ones’ lives, have and continues to induce, generate, produce and give birth hopefully endlessly to consciousness, out of trauma. We are the problem solving animal. We are the creative animal. We are the animal that creates solutions like it’s an art form.

Backing away from the body and into the evanescence of its own being, the fugitive chasing its tail, consciousness begins to explain itself, to define not only its states of mind but its own processes and steps of becoming, ever expanding and ever aware of itself again and again. This is the narrative underlayment that holds up, or rather, is behind the curtain of the ever present narratives we describe reality with as time passes, while looking backward.

In the act of splitting from ourselves in order to escape the trauma our bodies are, or were, experiencing, we sculpt thought patterns out of time through intention and awareness, and this is information, the content and context of which the inside of those limits and borders of patterns which are the sounds and textures and colors and shapes of those patterns, which are too information cut and geometrized out of time, are meaning, stories, experiences and the life of consciousness.

Interestingly, the subjective reality of free will and the freedom to choose ourselves and create our reality and morality affirms our individuation, under lament, without which we would be spread out completely, smeared with the rest of what and who we are presently aware of, without our own borders, within which is our freedom and responsibility. Often people who have stayed up too long, for too many days, find themselves trapped in this smearing and applying order to that disorder, forms of which are delusions and hallucinations, even as they order that which doesn’t necessarily require order in the first place. But like all manifestations of sensitivity, once achieved, stumbled into, forced to endure, there is no going back. With every expansion of the sphere of awareness, so is expansion of feeling, which the intellect detaches and disconnects from, but never completely, those feelings by being aware of them. And the cycle continues, refractions of that trauma and impetus to survive so long ago. And the cycle continues.

We have not advanced because, even as we have advanced so little, taken so little ground, we are superior or explorers on some conquest, but rather, because of the danger we face and have faced as one of the most vulnerable animals on the planet, full of fear and anxiety and dread. We puff up our egos not only to be the Source, but because it is a necessary illusion to cope with the horrors of the world. Most become spiritual. Others brush their tails. A few go mad.

We have not dominated the planet through intention, but rather through the need not only to learn, but to feel a sense of security, to the extent that we have endangered and wiped out whole other species, and critically endangered the planet’s biome. This security has allowed room for egos to flourish beyond the bounds of rationality and ethics on a large scale.

It is we prey animals, among others, that in such ways, that is, not only as pattern recognizers and creators, but also learners and seekers, as well as gifted problem solvers, that we are one of the negentropic forces of consciousness in the universe. Had it all been about conquest, will, domination and death and killing, the forces of entropy would have won and closed the system long ago.

So the universe learns about itself through endless refractions through conscious individuals learning about and witnessing themselves and each other endlessly. And the calculation unfolds. And the resolution becomes ever clearer.

8

Interestingly, the subjective reality of free will and the freedom to choose ourselves and create our reality and morality affirms our individuation, without which we would be spread out completely, smeared with the rest of what and who we are presently aware of, without our own borders, within which is our freedom and responsibility. Often people who have stayed up too long, for too many days, find themselves trapped in this smearing and applying order to that disorder, forms of which are delusions and hallucinations, even as they order that which doesn’t necessarily require order in the first place.

We have not advanced because, even as we have advanced so little, taken so little ground, we are superior or explorers on some conquest, but rather, because of the danger we face and have faced as one of the most vulnerable animals on the planet, full of fear and anxiety and dread. We puff up our egos not only to be the Source, but because it is a necessary illusion to cope with the horrors of the world. Most become spiritual. Others brush their tails. A few go mad.

We have not dominated the planet through intention, but rather through the need not only to learn, but to feel a sense of security, to the extent that we have endangered and wiped out whole other species, and critically endangered the planet’s biome. This security has allowed room for ego’s to flourish beyond the bounds of rationality and ethics on a large scale.

It is we prey animals, among others, that in such ways, that is, not only pattern recognizers and creators, but also learners and seekers, as well as gifted problem solvers, that we are one of the negentropic forces of consciousness in the universe. Had it all been about conquest, will, domination and death, the forces of entropy would have won and closed the system long ago.

So the universe learns about itself through endless refractions through conscious individuals learning about and witnessing themselves and each other endlessly.


9

Assumption 6:

Consciousness is coterminous with time and space.

Assumption 7:

As a natural phenomena of the universe, consciousness also utilizes time and space for its structure, which enables both the energy and information with which it feeds at the expense of its environment, namely, time and space, and in which it expels entropic waste.

Assumption 8:

We can say that consciousness expressed both internally and externally, are reflections in such ways of the laws, workings, forces and functions of the universe.

Assumption 9: 

Consciousness is thus a mirror and lens through which these scales emerge and the universe understands itself, or so we must assume.

Assumption 10:

The implications are that the universe is self-contained but ever expanding, and faster, and expresses itself in fractal perturbations, nodules, fingers and tendrils, and these types of filaments within and from its own forces are its extensions for its own self-discovery. And we call this consciousness.

We assume these filaments, which contain the tools to understand this universe, also have indeterminacy and inconsistency constraints, and just may allow for infinite burgeoning from the source, which creates information loops that are open ended and may flourish and whose spheres of awareness may ever expand because their environment is infinite, an environment which is never exhausted in its own expansion, if consciousness keeps its project intact.

Assumption 11:

It is possible that consciousness, its development and evolution and awareness, is itself a law and force of nature and the universe.

sources.title: Parables of Silence, Part 6, Take Away This Human Hand.txt
sources.content:
Parables of Silence, Chapter 6: Take Away This Human Hand
Are you looking for me? I am in the next seat. My shoulder is against yours. You will not find me in stupas, not in Indian shrine rooms, nor in synagogues, nor in cathedrals: not in masses, nor in kirtans, not in legs winding around your own neck, nor in eating nothing but vegetables. When you really look for me, you will see me instantly— you will find me in the tiniest house of time. Kabir says: Student, tell me what is God? He is the breath inside the breath.
Kabir, Breath
1
a)
There once was a god who thought night and day on the value of living,
until one day the god thought himself mortal.
Upon remembering that he was a god, he turned and looked to the god beside him, who said,
The illusion is far ahead.
b)
We can't help being thirsty moving toward the voice of water. Drinkers draw close to the mother: seekers, philosophers, scientists, mathematicians, Muslims, Christians, Jews, Buddhists, Hindus, shamans; everyone hears the intelligent sound and moves with thirst to meet it.
c)
After a most hallowed and endlessly non- echoed intake of breath I replied,
"I have been ignorant enough to think that self-awareness was a higher wisdom than listening."
A god smiled, a secret laughed.
I'll keep the beak that waits beneath my skin like rustling leaves a bit longer.
d)
“The Heaviest Burden. What if a demon crept after you into your loneliest loneliness some day or night, and said to you: "This life, as you live it at present, and have lived it, you must live it once more, and also innumerable times; and there will be nothing new in it, but every pain and every joy and every thought and every sigh, and all the unspeakably small and great in thy life must come to you again, and all in the same series and sequence - and similarly this spider and this moonlight among the trees, and similarly this moment, and I myself. The eternal sand-glass of existence will ever be turned once more, and you with it, you speck of dust!" - Would you not throw yourself down and gnash your teeth, and curse the demon that so spoke? Or have you once experienced a tremendous moment in which you would answer him: "You are a God, and never did I hear anything so divine!" If that thought acquired power over you as you are, it would transform you, and perhaps crush you; the question with regard to all and everything: "Do you want this once more, and also for innumerable times?" would lie as the heaviest burden upon your activity! Or, how would you have to become favourably inclined to yourself and to life, so as to long for nothing more ardently than for this last eternal sanctioning and sealing?”
Nietzsche, The Gay Science, 341
One of the ways that most haunts and thrills me is the idea of Spinoza's infinitely many kinds of existence. Rather than the Maimonist or Hegelian interpretation of Spinoza as denying the reality of change and time, I like to equate his notion of infinite existences in psi-ontological terms, where each choice and measurement actualizes the self amid infinite possibilities, thus creating infinitely more possibilities, until each choice of one's self narrows continually the future unto death. (Though in such terms infinite possibilities could all be actualized infinitely in infinite universes.)
In The Gay Science, Nietzsche posits a demon looking over you, telling you that as you have lived in the past and as you live in the present, so you will over again and again and forever. Contrary to the common interpretation of eternal recurrence as strong determinism, I read its “as you live now,” as a form of radical freedom, where one's choices are choices for eternity and bear that much personal responsibility, one of the things that connects Nietzsche to the Existentialists. The great challenge is saying "yes" to this responsibility, embracing one's fate that one must choose in radical freedom, and this amor fati is another way a person fully becomes themselves.
As I come to be more myself through self-acceptance, I also become more myself by choosing myself from infinite possibilities as if I were choosing for eternity. And though time and prior choices have narrowed possibilities, the good of civilization can help me to actualize myself, a part of which is in turn helping civilization.
As I grow older, among the age spots on my face, the aching, the balding, the poorer eyesight, I am more becoming myself, while still having the freedom and responsibility to choose myself in the future.
2
a)
Neil deGrasse Tyson has spoken of the possibility that there may be another species who have a 1% genetic difference of a higher order than we, who have, and in the same manner, a 1% genetic difference apart from Chimpanzees. There we can posit a hypothetical, namely, that the same physical consequents of our evolved minds, that difference in the ability to create and embody and express so many varied and exuberant facial expressions, might seem to some advanced species as barbaric, limits which capture and imprison the internal working of our minds physically. Perhaps they would regard themselves and their possible mute expressions as mental energy transcending the physical, as energy charged enough will blur and shine so bright it deludes the boundaries and shapes of its physical comportment and fettle and guise.
b)
Emotion, which is suffering, ceases to be suffering as soon as our minds form clear and precise pictures of it. Or so said Spinoza. Interestingly, Spinoza, like most thinkers (almost everyone alive for tens of thousands of years), thought that emotions were associated and conjoined with the body, but that consciousness was separate, but still part of the whole, just simply in a different mode or manifestation of the mind of God.  And in some ways I think he was right, but that might be deep conditioning from our species linguistic and mythological and metaphysical assumptions, a peak into which are Jung’s shadows and Dawkins’ memes, both of which have been totally corrupted from their initial forms, and who may in fact be saying the same thing from different perspectives.
I’ve given this a lot of thought.
